Additional check: Other code that uses polygon vertices directly
- I searched the repository for uses of poly.xy and found only Slider, RangeSlider, and an example poly_editor that manipulates poly.xy directly. Example code (poly_editor) expects poly.xy to be mutable and sometimes expects the last vertex to be the closing vertex; but that example already handled poly.xy length carefully (e.g., tests in that example use len(self.poly.xy) and indexing; it even has logic like if self._ind == len(self.poly.xy) - 1 then set self.poly.xy[0] etc). The fix keeps behavior compatible (we only write to existing indices or replace poly.xy if too short), so examples continue to work.

Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- RangeSlider.set_val assumed the polygon returned by ax.axvspan/ax.axhspan ended with an explicit duplicate closing vertex (i.e., poly.xy length >= 5). However, when the span has zero width (valinit[0] == valinit[1]), the polygon's last vertex equals the first, and patches.Polygon.set_xy detects that and does not append another duplicate closing vertex. So poly.xy has length 4; RangeSlider.set_val tries to write to xy[4] and raises IndexError.

Every location that needs to change (complete list)
- Primary change: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py — RangeSlider.set_val: make it robust to poly.xy having 4 or 5 vertices (or less).

No other code locations must be changed:
- Slider.set_val (single handle) is not buggy here; leave unchanged.
- patches.Polygon behavior is correct and should remain unchanged — the logic to append a closing vertex only when first != last is intentional and should remain.

For the RangeSlider.set_val change — what the current code does, what assumption it encodes, and what the fix should be:
- Current code (excerpt):

    xy = self.poly.xy
    if self.orientation == "vertical":
        xy[0] = .25, val[0]
        xy[1] = .25, val[1]
        xy[2] = .75, val[1]
        xy[3] = .75, val[0]
        xy[4] = .25, val[0]
    else:
        xy[0] = val[0], .25
        xy[1] = val[0], .75
        xy[2] = val[1], .75
        xy[3] = val[1], .25
        xy[4] = val[0], .25

  - Assumption encoded: poly.xy has at least 5 vertices (index 4 exists).
  - Fix: write only to indices that exist; for n == 4 only write indices 0..3; for n >=5 write indices 0..4; if n < 4 rebuild polygon with correct 4 core vertices.

Any hardcoded values needing to become dynamic:
- None need to be made dynamic. The constants .25 and .75 are layout parameters (fractions of axis space) and remain valid.

Any methods that need to be added or overridden:
- None required. No additional parent methods need overriding.

Suggested patch (concise)
- Replace the RangeSlider.set_val function in lib/matplotlib/widgets.py with the robust implementation shown above. This is the only change required to fix the IndexError illustrated in the bug report while preserving expected behavior in the common case (poly with closing vertex).

Rationale and tests
- The test case from the bug report uses valinit=[0.0, 0.0]. After the patch, constructing RangeSlider with valinit equal endpoints will no longer raise IndexError; instead RangeSlider.set_val will update the four polygon vertices correctly (no attempt to write xy[4]).
- Additional tests to run:
  - The original repro script in the bug report — should no longer raise IndexError.
  - RangeSlider with valinit different endpoints (normal case) — should behave exactly as before (polygon with 5 vertices, all 5 indices updated).
  - Reset and drag interactions: exercise RangeSlider.reset(), set_min(), set_max(), and interactive dragging — should behave as before.
  - Existing examples which manipulate poly.xy directly (e.g., examples/event_handling/poly_editor.py) — should still work.

Notes about alternative fixes
- One could attempt to always recreate self.poly with 5 vertices (e.g., by explicitly setting self.poly._closed or passing a closed vertex), but that modifies Polygon construction semantics and risks interfering with other code expecting Polygon to deduplicate closing vertex when the first and last are equal. The minimal and safest fix is to make RangeSlider.set_val robust to the two possible polygon vertex counts produced by patches.Polygon.

If you want, I can:
- Generate a precise git-style patch (diff) for lib/matplotlib/widgets.py with the replacement function to apply.
- Run through more uses of poly.xy in examples and tests to ensure compatibility.

Summary (short)
- Root cause: RangeSlider.set_val assumed poly.xy always had 5 vertices and wrote to xy[4]; when initial span was zero-width, Polygon left poly.xy with 4 vertices and IndexError occurs.
- Fix: Make RangeSlider.set_val check the number of vertices and only write indices that exist (handle n==4 and n>=5; reconstruct if n<4).
- Only file to change: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py (RangeSlider.set_val). This addresses the problem in all call sites of set_val (constructor, reset, events).
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: RangeSlider.set_val unconditionally writes to xy[4] (e.g. the line "xy[4] = val[0], .25"), assuming the polygon stored in self.poly.xy always has a duplicated closing vertex (length >= 5). That assumption is false when the span has zero width (valinit[0] == valinit[1]) — in that case the polygon's last vertex equals the first and patches.Polygon does NOT append a duplicate closing vertex, so poly.xy has length 4 and xy[4] raises IndexError. (source: Checkpoint 1d, Checkpoint 3a)

- Root cause location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py — RangeSlider.set_val (method). The failing statement is exactly: xy[4] = val[0], .25 (IndexError: index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4). (source: Checkpoint 1d)

- Proposed fix strategy: Make RangeSlider.set_val robust to the actual number of vertices in self.poly.xy. Inspect n = xy.shape[0]; if n == 4 update indices 0..3 only; if n >= 5 update indices 0..4; if n < 4 reconstruct a correct 4-vertex polygon. This fixes the constructor call (self.set_val(valinit)) and all call sites that use set_val (reset, event updates). Change only lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::RangeSlider.set_val. (source: Checkpoint 4)

- Hardcoded constants: .25 and .75 are layout fractions used to position the rectangle corners and remain correct; the real problematic “hardcoded” assumption was the index access xy[4], not a magic numeric layout constant. No layout constants need to be made dynamic. (source: Checkpoint 3a, Checkpoint 4b)

- Methods to add/override: None required. The fix is limited to making the existing RangeSlider.set_val handle both 4- and 5-vertex polygons. (source: Checkpoint 4e)

- Gaps in the investigation: Static code inspection across the codebase was done, but no runtime tests were executed. The agent did not run the repro or unit tests to validate the patch; it also did not check older/newer Matplotlib releases for differing Polygon behavior (though the relevant code in patches.Polygon was inspected). These are low-risk gaps but should be covered by running the repro and adding a unit test. (source: Checkpoint 4 — gap note)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Immediate cause — out-of-bounds write in RangeSlider.set_val
What was discovered and why it matters:
RangeSlider.set_val assumes self.poly.xy has a closing vertex and executes xy[4] = ... unconditionally, causing IndexError when poly.xy has only 4 vertices. File: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py, class RangeSlider, method set_val. This is the direct crash point from the stack trace. (source: Checkpoint 1d)

### Finding 2: Why poly.xy can have 4 vertices (patches.Polygon behavior)
What was discovered and why it matters:
patches.Polygon.__init__/set_xy appends the first vertex only when self._closed is True AND the first and last vertices differ; it checks (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any() and only then appends the first point. Therefore when the rectangle span is zero-width (first == last), Polygon leaves xy length == 4. File: lib/matplotlib/patches.py, class Polygon, method set_xy; the relevant test is exactly "(xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()". This explains the 4-vertex case that triggers the IndexError in RangeSlider.set_val. (source: Checkpoint 3a)

### Finding 3: Repro condition — valinit with equal endpoints produces the bug
What was discovered and why it matters:
The user repro passes valinit=[0.0, 0.0]; when axvspan/axhspan are called with identical endpoints, the created verts are [(v,0),(v,1),(v,1),(v,0)] so first == last and Polygon does not append a closing vertex; poly.xy has shape (4,2) and xy[4] is out of range. This concrete value trace reproduces the IndexError. (source: Checkpoint 1d & 3a)

### Finding 4: Minimal, correct fix — make set_val conditional on number of vertices
What was discovered and why it matters:
The correct fix is to modify lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::RangeSlider.set_val to inspect n = self.poly.xy.shape[0] and:
- if n >= 5: update indices 0..4 (existing behavior),
- elif n == 4: update indices 0..3 only (do not write xy[4]),
- elif n < 4: rebuild self.poly.xy from computed verts (fallback).
This preserves visual geometry (.25/.75 insets) and addresses all call sites (constructor, reset, events). (source: Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 5: Scope — only widgets.py needs edit; do not change Polygon
What was discovered and why it matters:
No changes to patches.Polygon are necessary or desirable; Polygon's behavior (not duplicating closing vertex when first==last) is intentional. The only required change is in RangeSlider.set_val (lib/matplotlib/widgets.py). Other classes (Slider) use different vertex indexing and are unaffected. (source: Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 6: Missing runtime validation / tests
What was discovered and why it matters:
Investigation was static; the agent did not execute the repro or run unit tests. Add a unit test reproducing valinit with identical endpoints and the interactive update path (constructor, reset, drag) to prevent regressions. (source: Checkpoint 4 — gap)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py:RangeSlider.set_val
- Wrong assumption: The method assumes self.poly.xy always contains a duplicate closing vertex and therefore that index 4 exists (it executes xy[4] = ... unconditionally).
- Evidence:
  - Crash stack trace from the bug: IndexError raised at "lib/matplotlib/widgets.py", line 915, in set_val -> "xy[4] = val[0], .25" leading to "IndexError: index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4". (source: Checkpoint 1d)
  - Current code excerpt (original problematic lines):
    - vertical branch: xy[0] = .25, val[0]; xy[1] = .25, val[1]; xy[2] = .75, val[1]; xy[3] = .75, val[0]; xy[4] = .25, val[0]
    - horizontal branch: xy[0] = val[0], .25; xy[1] = val[0], .75; xy[2] = val[1], .75; xy[3] = val[1], .25; xy[4] = val[0], .25
  - Concrete repro values: valinit = [0.0, 0.0] -> polygon verts from axvspan are [(v,0),(v,1),(v,1),(v,0)] so last == first, so poly.xy length == 4. (source: Checkpoint 1d & 3a)
- Related locations:
  - Slider.set_val (lib/matplotlib/widgets.py) uses different index pattern (xy[1..3]) and is not changed.
  - Any code that reads/writes self.poly.xy (e.g., examples/event_handling/poly_editor.py) — they already handle poly.xy length variations in their code; RangeSlider must be made similarly robust. (source: search for poly.xy usages, Checkpoint 3d)

### Location: lib/matplotlib/patches.py:Polygon.set_xy (and __init__)
- Wrong assumption (as relevant to RangeSlider): None — Polygon intentionally does not append a duplicate closing vertex when the first and last vertices are equal; RangeSlider incorrectly assumed otherwise.
- Evidence:
  - set_xy contains logic: if self._closed and (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any(): append xy[0] to xy. That exact condition means equal first/last => no appended vertex. (source: Checkpoint 3a)
  - Therefore when verts are [(v,0),(v,1),(v,1),(v,0)] the test is False and poly.xy remains 4 vertices.
- Related locations:
  - axes._axes.axvspan/axhspan construct Polygon with verts = (xmin,ymin),(xmin,ymax),(xmax,ymax),(xmax,ymin). For zero-width x coords these produce the equal-first-last situation. (source: Checkpoint 3a)

### Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:axvspan / axhspan (creation of polygon verts)
- Wrong assumption (as relevant): none — these functions correctly create a 4-vertex rectangle representation: verts = (xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin).
- Evidence:
  - axvspan builds verts = (xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin) and calls mpatches.Polygon(verts, **kwargs). When xmin == xmax, verts has matching first and last vertices so Polygon.set_xy may not append a closing vertex. (source: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py, lines building verts)
- Related locations:
  - RangeSlider.__init__ calls ax.axvspan/axhspan with valinit endpoints; if valinit endpoints equal, the 4-vertex polygon situation is produced and triggers the RangeSlider.set_val failure unless set_val is made robust. (source: Checkpoint 3a & 1d)

If you want, I can produce the exact git-style patch (diff) for lib/matplotlib/widgets.py that replaces RangeSlider.set_val with the conditional implementation shown in the investigation and add a unit test that reproduces valinit with identical endpoints.

Removed prescriptive or fix-oriented text from RootCause (no implementation guidance or patch suggestions).
Simplified Description to state the observable issue concisely and removed implementation-level fix details from Description and ExpectedBehavior.
Kept StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, and ObservedBehavior focused on user-visible behavior and reproduction steps.
Clarified that Polygon.set_xy appends a closing vertex only when the first and last input vertices differ (quoted exact test behavior).
reviewer_evidence: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py (RangeSlider.set_val): the method reads xy = self.poly.xy and unconditionally writes xy[0]..xy[4] in both orientation branches (see assignments around set_val; xy[4] = .25, val[0] and xy[4] = val[0], .25). (widgets.py lines ~900-913 in the checked commit).

lib/matplotlib/patches.py (Polygon.set_xy): the method appends the first vertex only when self._closed and the provided first and last vertices differ; exact code: if self._closed: if nverts == 1 or nverts > 1 and (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any(): xy = np.concatenate([xy, [xy[0]]]). This means no duplicate closing vertex is appended when the first and last are equal. (patches.py lines ~1147-1156).

lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py (axvspan/axhspan): these functions build verts = [(xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin)] and pass them to mpatches.Polygon. When xmin == xmax the first and last vertices are identical, producing a 4-vertex polygon input. (axes/_axes.py lines ~994).

Stack/trace evidence from the original report: the IndexError observed ("index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4") is raised at the xy[4] assignment in RangeSlider.set_val, matching the code location where xy[4] is written (widgets.py).

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft enhanced report against the source code and the investigation summary.

Planned checks and expectations (performed already):
- Read RangeSlider.set_val in lib/matplotlib/widgets.py to confirm the exact xy index writes and confirm the xy[4] writes are present (expect to find xy[4] = ... in both branches).
- Read Polygon.set_xy in lib/matplotlib/patches.py to confirm when Polygon appends the closing vertex and the exact condition used (expect to find a check comparing first and last vertex and concatenation).
- Read axvspan/axhspan in lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py to confirm the verts layout used (expect 4-vertex rectangle construction that can have identical first and last vertex when xmin==xmax).
- Search widget tests to confirm there is no unit test covering the equal-endpoint valinit case (expect tests for RangeSlider but not for identical endpoints).

I performed those reads; below are the findings and how they map to the draft report.

1) RangeSlider.set_val (lib/matplotlib/widgets.py)
- What I looked for: the lines in set_val that write into self.poly.xy and in particular any unconditional write to index 4.
- Evidence from file: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py shows RangeSlider.set_val writes to xy indices, including an unconditional xy[4] assignment in both orientation branches:
  - Vertical branch writes xy[0]..xy[4]; the write to xy[4] is present (xy[4] = .25, val[0]).
  - Horizontal branch writes xy[0]..xy[4]; the write to xy[4] is present (xy[4] = val[0], .25).
  - (See widgets.py lines ~900-913; xy[4] assignments at lines 906 and 912 in the checked commit.)
- Conclusion: The draft claim that RangeSlider.set_val unconditionally writes xy[4] is accurate and confirmed.

2) Polygon.set_xy behavior (lib/matplotlib/patches.py)
- What I looked for: the logic that determines whether a duplicate closing vertex is appended when the polygon is closed.
- Evidence from file: lib/matplotlib/patches.py::Polygon.set_xy contains:
  - The code converts xy to an array and computes nverts.
  - If self._closed: if nverts == 1 or (nverts > 1 and (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()): xy = np.concatenate([xy, [xy[0]]]) — i.e., append first vertex only when either a single vertex was provided or when the first and last differ (checked via (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()).
  - (See patches.py lines ~1147-1156 in the checked commit.)
- Conclusion: The draft's characterization that Polygon only appends the closing vertex when first and last differ is correct (the code uses (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()). The code also handles the single-vertex special case, which is consistent with the intent. The draft's explanation is supported by the code.

3) axvspan/axhspan verts construction (lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py)
- What I looked for: how axvspan/axhspan construct the rectangle vertices passed to Polygon.
- Evidence from file: axvspan constructs verts = [(xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin)] (four vertices) and passes that list to mpatches.Polygon(verts, **kwargs).
  - When xmin == xmax (zero-width span), the first and last vertices are identical, so the list's first and last elements match.
  - (See axes/_axes.py lines ~994.)
- Conclusion: The draft's description that axvspan/axhspan produce a 4-vertex rectangle and that when endpoints are equal the first and last vertex are identical is correct and supported by the code.

4) Reproduction trace and resulting IndexError
- What I checked: that when Polygon.set_xy receives a 4-vertex list with first==last, it will not append a closing vertex, hence poly.xy has length 4, and RangeSlider.set_val's xy[4] write will raise IndexError.
- Evidence: Combining 1–3:
  - axvspan provides 4 vertices which are identical at first and last when xmin==xmax.
  - Polygon.set_xy therefore does not append a closing vertex in this case.
  - RangeSlider.set_val then writes xy[4] unconditionally; if poly.xy has shape (4, 2) this write is out of bounds and raises IndexError.
  - The original user stack trace included the IndexError from widgets.py at the xy[4] assignment (matches the code location).
- Conclusion: The causal chain presented in the draft report is correct and supported by the code and the user-observed stack trace.

5) Test coverage / validation gap
- What I checked: whether tests exercise the equal-endpoint initialization of RangeSlider.
- Evidence: tests/test_widgets.py contains test_range_slider that tests RangeSlider initialization with valinit [0.1, 0.34] and exercises set_val with various values. There is no test that constructs RangeSlider with valinit where both values are equal (e.g., [v, v]) in the test_range_slider function.
  - (See tests/test_widgets.py lines ~1096–1112.)
- Conclusion: The investigation's statement that there was no test covering the identical-endpoint case (and thus the behavior was not covered by unit tests) is supported by the test file inspection.

6) Any claims that are not supported or need softening
- The draft stated in RootCause point 2 that Polygon.append behavior is "governed by the test (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()." That is correct, but the full code includes a special-case for nverts == 1; I note this but it does not change the diagnosis here. I will keep the RootCause statement focused on the assumption mismatch (RangeSlider expects a duplicate closing vertex; Polygon only appends one when first and last differ).
- The draft contained several enumerated items including proposed fixes and mention of "hardcoded constants" (.25/.75) and a suggested exact fix. Per the guidelines, RootCause must state the incorrect assumptions and the locations only (no fix suggestions). I will therefore remove prescriptive language from RootCause and keep only the diagnostic statements + formula relationship about how updates to poly.xy must depend on the polygon vertex count.
- The draft's Title, Description, StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior are aligned with findings; I will only slightly rewrite to ensure each field remains within its allowed scope (no fixes in Description/RootCause; Expected/Observed only user-visible behavior).

7) Completeness check for other locations sharing the same root-cause pattern
- The investigation mentions Slider.set_val uses different indexing and is unaffected. I checked widgets.Slider.set_val and confirmed it updates indices 1..3 (different pattern) and so does not assume index 4 exists unconditionally.
- No other widget in widgets.py writes to xy[4] unconditionally for the same pattern. I searched for other uses of .poly.xy writes and did not find any that match the same incorrect assumption. The key related code locations that must be in RootCause (all with equal weight) are:
  - lib/matplotlib/widgets.py :: RangeSlider.set_val (unconditional xy[4] write).
  - lib/matplotlib/patches.py :: Polygon.set_xy (closing-vertex append behavior).
  - lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py :: axvspan/axhspan (4-vertex rectangle construction producing first==last for zero-width span).
- These three locations form the full root cause chain for the observed IndexError and are all represented in my revised RootCause.

Summary of verification
- Draft claim that RangeSlider.set_val unconditionally writes xy[4] — verified.
- Draft claim that Polygon only appends the closing vertex when first and last differ — verified (exact expression: (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()).
- Draft claim that axvspan/axhspan create 4-vertex rectangle verts that lead to first==last when endpoints equal — verified.
- Draft claim regarding lack of test coverage for identical endpoints — supported by inspection of tests.
- Items in the draft recommending a specific code fix or providing implementation changes are valid engineering suggestions but must be removed from RootCause (the report must be a diagnosis only). I will remove prescriptive text and move the content to "changes" as a description of the editorial changes I made.

bug_report.Title: RangeSlider.__init__/set_val crashes with IndexError when valinit endpoints are equal
bug_report.Description: Creating a RangeSlider with identical initial endpoints (for example valinit=[v, v]) raises an IndexError during initialization because RangeSlider.set_val writes to a polygon vertex index that does not exist when the underlying Polygon has only four vertices.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/widgets.py :: RangeSlider.set_val assumes self.poly.xy always contains a duplicate closing vertex and unconditionally writes to index 4 (the code assigns xy[4] in both orientation branches). This encodes the assumption that self.poly.xy.shape[0] >= 5. (Location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py, RangeSlider.set_val)

2) lib/matplotlib/patches.py :: Polygon.set_xy appends a duplicate closing vertex only when the polygon is closed and the first and last input vertices differ. The code concatenates the first vertex when self._closed and (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any() is True (special case: nverts==1 is also handled). Therefore, when the first and last vertices are equal, Polygon.set_xy does not append a closing vertex and poly.xy can have length 4 instead of 5. (Location: lib/matplotlib/patches.py, Polygon.set_xy)

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py :: axvspan/axhspan build rectangle verts as four points: (xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin). When xmin == xmax (or ymin == ymax for the horizontal case) the first and last vertices are identical, producing a 4-vertex polygon input where Polygon.set_xy will not append a closing vertex. This is the concrete scenario that yields poly.xy.shape == (4, 2). (Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py, axvspan/axhspan)

4) Diagnostic relationship: RangeSlider.set_val must not assume a duplicated closing vertex exists. Updates to self.poly.xy must be conditional on the actual vertex count n = self.poly.xy.shape[0]: if n == 4 only indices 0..3 may be updated; if n >= 5 indices 0..4 may be updated; if n < 4 the polygon vertex list is incomplete and a valid set of vertices must be constructed. The bug is the hard assumption that index 4 always exists, which leads to an out-of-bounds write when poly.xy has only 4 rows. (Locations: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py :: RangeSlider.set_val; lib/matplotlib/patches.py :: Polygon.set_xy; lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py :: axvspan/axhspan)
StepsToReproduce: Start Python with matplotlib installed.
Create an axes and construct a RangeSlider whose initial endpoints are equal, e.g.:
slider = RangeSlider(ax, 'Threshold', data.min(), data.max(), valinit=[0.0, 0.0])
Observe that RangeSlider.__init__ calls self.set_val(valinit) and this raises an IndexError.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Constructing a RangeSlider with user-specified initial endpoints (including equal endpoints) succeeds without raising an exception. The widget initializes and displays the requested initial min and max values (including the zero-width range case).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Constructing a RangeSlider with identical initial endpoints raises an IndexError during RangeSlider.set_val (e.g. "IndexError: index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4"), because the code writes to xy[4] when the polygon vertex array has only four rows.

method_cache.lib/matplotlib/artist.py:
.. note::
            This method is intended to be overridden by artist subclasses.
            As an end-user of Matplotlib you will most likely not call this
            method yourself.

        Cursor data can be used by Artists to provide additional context
        information for a given event. The default implementation just returns
        *None*.

        Subclasses can override the method and return arbitrary data. However,
        when doing so, they must ensure that `.format_cursor_data` can convert
        the data to a string representation.

        The only current use case is displaying the z-value of an `.AxesImage`
        in the status bar of a plot window, while moving the mouse.

        Parameters
        ----------
        event : `matplotlib.backend_bases.MouseEvent`

        See Also
        --------
        format_cursor_data

        """
        return None

    def format_cursor_data(self, data):
        """
        Return a string representation of *data*.

        .. note::
            This method is intended to be overridden by artist subclasses.
            As an end-user of Matplotlib you will most likely not call this
            method yourself.

        The default implementation converts ints and floats and arrays of ints
        and floats into a comma-separated string enclosed in square brackets,
        unless the artist has an associated colorbar, in which case scalar
        values are formatted using the colorbar's formatter.

        See Also
        --------
        get_cursor_data
        """
        if np.ndim(data) == 0 and isinstance(self, ScalarMappable):
            # This block logically belongs to ScalarMappable, but can't be
            # implemented in it because most ScalarMappable subclasses inherit
            # from Artist first and from ScalarMappable second, so
            # Artist.format_cursor_data would always have precedence over
            # ScalarMappable.format_cursor_data.
            n = self.cmap.N
            if np.ma.getmask(data):
                return "[]"
            normed = self.norm(data)
            if np.isfinite(normed):
                # Midpoints of neighboring color intervals.
                neighbors = self.norm.inverse(
                    (int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
                delta = abs(neighbors - data).max()
                g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta)

class ScalarMappable:
    """
    A mixin class to map scalar data to RGBA.

    The ScalarMappable applies data normalization before returning RGBA colors
    from the given colormap.
    """

    def __init__(self, norm=None, cmap=None):
        """

        Parameters
        ----------
        norm : `matplotlib.colors.Normalize` (or subclass thereof)
            The normalizing object which scales data, typically into the
            interval ``[0, 1]``.
            If *None*, *norm* defaults to a *colors.Normalize* object which
            initializes its scaling based on the first data processed.
        cmap : str or `~matplotlib.colors.Colormap`
            The colormap used to map normalized data values to RGBA colors.
        """
        self._A = None
        self._norm = None  # So that the setter knows we're initializing.
        self.set_norm(norm)  # The Normalize instance of this ScalarMappable.
        self.cmap = None  # So that the setter knows we're initializing.
        self.set_cmap(cmap)  # The Colormap instance of this ScalarMappable.
        #: The last colorbar associated with this ScalarMappable. May be None.
        self.colorbar = None
        self.callbacks = cbook.CallbackRegistry(signals=["changed"])

        property(lambda self: self.callbacks))

    def _scale_norm(self, norm, vmin, vmax):

        Helper for initial scaling.

        Used by public functions that create a ScalarMappable and support
        parameters *vmin*, *vmax* and *norm*. This makes sure that a *norm*
        will take precedence over *vmin*, *vmax*.

        Note that this method does not set the norm.
        """
        if vmin is not None or vmax is not None:
            self.set_clim(vmin, vmax)
            if norm is not None:
                raise ValueError(
                    "Passing parameters norm and vmin/vmax simultaneously is "
                    "not supported. Please pass vmin/vmax directly to the "
                    "norm when creating it.")

        # always resolve the autoscaling so we have concrete limits
        # rather than deferring to draw time.
        self.autoscale_None()

    def to_rgba(self, x, alpha=None, bytes=False, norm=True):
        """
        Return a normalized rgba array corresponding to *x*.

        In the normal case, *x* is a 1D or 2D sequence of scalars, and
        the corresponding ndarray of rgba values will be returned,
        based on the norm and colormap set for this ScalarMappable.

        There is one special case, for handling images that are already
        rgb or rgba, such as might have been read from an image file.
        If *x* is an ndarray with 3 dimensions,
        and the last dimension is either 3 or 4, then it will be
        treated as an rgb or rgba array, and no mapping will be done.
        The array can be uint8, or it can be floating point with
        values in the 0-1 range; otherwise a ValueError will be raised.
        If it is a masked array, the mask will be ignored.
        If the last dimension is 3, the *alpha* kwarg (defaulting to 1)
        will be used to fill in the transparency.  If the last dimension
        is 4, the *alpha* kwarg is ignored; it does not
        replace the pre-existing alpha.  A ValueError will be raised
        if the third dimension is other than 3 or 4.

        In either case, if *bytes* is *False* (default), the rgba
        array will be floats in the 0-1 range; if it is *True*,
        the returned rgba array will be uint8 in the 0 to 255 range.

        If norm is False, no normalization of the input data is
        performed, and it is assumed to be in the range (0-1).

        """
        # First check for special case, image input:
        try:
            if x.ndim == 3:
                if x.shape[2] == 3:
                    if alpha is None:
                        alpha = 1
                    if x.dtype == np.uint8:
                        alpha = np.uint8(alpha * 255)
                    m, n = x.shape[:2]
                    xx = np.empty(shape=(m, n, 4), dtype=x.dtype)
                    xx[:, :, :3] = x
                    xx[:, :, 3] = alpha
                elif x.shape[2] == 4:
                    xx = x
                else:
                    raise ValueError("Third dimension must be 3 or 4")
                if xx.dtype.kind == 'f':
                    if norm and (xx.max() > 1 or xx.min() < 0):
                        raise ValueError("Floating point image RGB values "
                                         "must be in the 0..1 range.")
                    if bytes:
                        xx = (xx * 255).astype(np.uint8)
                elif xx.dtype == np.uint8:
                    if not bytes:
                        xx = xx.astype(np.float32) / 255
                else:
                    raise ValueError("Image RGB array must be uint8 or "
                                     "floating point; found %s" % xx.dtype)
                return xx
        except AttributeError:
            # e.g., x is not an ndarray; so try mapping it
            pass

        x = ma.asarray(x)
        if norm:
            x = self.norm(x)

    def set_array(self, A):
        """
        Set the value array from array-like *A*.

        Parameters
        ----------
        A : array-like or None
            The values that are mapped to colors.

            The base class `.ScalarMappable` does not make any assumptions on
            the dimensionality and shape of the value array *A*.
        """
        if A is None:
            self._A = None
            return

        A = cbook.safe_masked_invalid(A, copy=True)
        if not np.can_cast(A.dtype, float, "same_kind"):
            raise TypeError(f"Image data of dtype {A.dtype} cannot be "
                            "converted to float")

        self._A = A

    def get_array(self):
        """
        Return the array of values, that are mapped to colors.

        The base class `.ScalarMappable` does not make any assumptions on
        the dimensionality and shape of the array.
        """
        return self._A

    def get_cmap(self):
        """Return the `.Colormap` instance."""
        return self.cmap

    def get_clim(self):
        """
        Return the values (min, max) that are mapped to the colormap limits.
        """
        return self.norm.vmin, self.norm.vmax

    def set_clim(self, vmin=None, vmax=None):
        """
        Set the norm limits for image scaling.

        Parameters
        ----------
        vmin, vmax : float
             The limits.

             The limits may also be passed as a tuple (*vmin*, *vmax*) as a
             single positional argument.

             .. ACCEPTS: (vmin: float, vmax: float)
        """
        # If the norm's limits are updated self.changed() will be called
        # through the callbacks attached to the norm
        if vmax is None:
            try:
                vmin, vmax = vmin
            except (TypeError, ValueError):
                pass
        if vmin is not None:
            self.norm.vmin = colors._sanitize_extrema(vmin)
        if vmax is not None:
            self.norm.vmax = colors._sanitize_extrema(vmax)

    def get_alpha(self):
        """
        Returns
        -------
        float
            Always returns 1.
        """
        # This method is intended to be overridden by Artist sub-classes
        return 1.

    def set_cmap(self, cmap):
        """
        Set the colormap for luminance data.

        Parameters
        ----------
        cmap : `.Colormap` or str or None
        """
        in_init = self.cmap is None
        cmap = get_cmap(cmap)
        self.cmap = cmap
        if not in_init:
            self.changed()  # Things are not set up properly yet.

    @property
    def norm(self):
        return self._norm

    @norm.setter
    def norm(self, norm):
        _api.check_isinstance((colors.Normalize, None), norm=norm)
        if norm is None:
            norm = colors.Normalize()

        if norm is self.norm:
            # We aren't updating anything
            return

        in_init = self.norm is None
        # Remove the current callback and connect to the new one
        if not in_init:
            self.norm.callbacks.disconnect(self._id_norm)
        self._norm = norm
        self._id_norm = self.norm.callbacks.connect('changed',
                                                    self.changed)
        if not in_init:
            self.changed()

    def set_norm(self, norm):
        """
        Set the normalization instance.

        Parameters
        ----------
        norm : `.Normalize` or None

        Notes
        -----
        If there are any colorbars using the mappable for this norm, setting
        the norm of the mappable will reset the norm, locator, and formatters
        on the colorbar to default.
        """
        self.norm = norm

    def autoscale(self):
        """
        Autoscale the scalar limits on the norm instance using the
        current array
        """
        if self._A is None:
            raise TypeError('You must first set_array for mappable')
        # If the norm's limits are updated self.changed() will be called
        # through the callbacks attached to the norm
        self.norm.autoscale(self._A)

    def autoscale_None(self):
        """
        Autoscale the scalar limits on the norm instance using the
        current array, changing only limits that are None
        """
        if self._A is None:
            raise TypeError('You must first set_array for mappable')
        # If the norm's limits are updated self.changed() will be called
        # through the callbacks attached to the norm
        self.norm.autoscale_None(self._A)

    def changed(self):
        """
        Call this whenever the mappable is changed to notify all the
        callbackSM listeners to the 'changed' signal.
        """
        self.callbacks.process('changed', self)
        self.stale = True

def test_figimage(suppressComposite):
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(2, 2), dpi=100)
    fig.suppressComposite = suppressComposite
    x, y = np.ix_(np.arange(100) / 100.0, np.arange(100) / 100)
    z = np.sin(x**2 + y**2 - x*y)
    c = np.sin(20*x**2 + 50*y**2)

    fig.figimage(img, xo=0, yo=0, origin='lower')
    fig.figimage(img[::-1, :], xo=0, yo=100, origin='lower')
    fig.figimage(img[:, ::-1], xo=100, yo=0, origin='lower')
    fig.figimage(img[::-1, ::-1], xo=100, yo=100, origin='lower')

@pytest.mark.parametrize("fmt", ["png", "jpg", "jpeg", "tiff"])
def test_imsave(fmt):
    has_alpha = fmt not in ["jpg", "jpeg"]

    # The goal here is that the user can specify an output logical DPI
    # for the image, but this will not actually add any extra pixels
    # to the image, it will merely be used for metadata purposes.

    # So we do the traditional case (dpi == 1), and the new case (dpi
    # == 100) and read the resulting PNG files back in and make sure
    # the data is 100% identical.
    np.random.seed(1)
    # The height of 1856 pixels was selected because going through creating an
    # actual dpi=100 figure to save the image to a Pillow-provided format would
    # cause a rounding error resulting in a final image of shape 1855.
    data = np.random.rand(1856, 2)

    buff_dpi1 = io.BytesIO()
    plt.imsave(buff_dpi1, data, format=fmt, dpi=1)

    buff_dpi100 = io.BytesIO()
    plt.imsave(buff_dpi100, data, format=fmt, dpi=100)

    buff_dpi1.seek(0)
    arr_dpi1 = plt.imread(buff_dpi1, format=fmt)

    buff_dpi100.seek(0)
    arr_dpi100 = plt.imread(buff_dpi100, format=fmt)

    assert arr_dpi1.shape == (1856, 2, 3 + has_alpha)
    assert arr_dpi100.shape == (1856, 2, 3 + has_alpha)

    assert_array_equal(arr_dpi1, arr_dpi100)

def test_imsave_color_alpha():
    # Test that imsave accept arrays with ndim=3 where the third dimension is
    # color and alpha without raising any exceptions, and that the data is
    # acceptably preserved through a save/read roundtrip.
    np.random.seed(1)

    for origin in ['lower', 'upper']:
        data = np.random.rand(16, 16, 4)
        buff = io.BytesIO()
        plt.imsave(buff, data, origin=origin, format="png")

        buff.seek(0)
        arr_buf = plt.imread(buff)

        # Recreate the float -> uint8 conversion of the data
        # We can only expect to be the same with 8 bits of precision,
        # since that's what the PNG file used.
        data = (255*data).astype('uint8')
        if origin == 'lower':
            data = data[::-1]
        arr_buf = (255*arr_buf).astype('uint8')

        assert_array_equal(data, arr_buf)

    ax.set_ylim(0, 3)

    ax = fig_test.subplots()

    ax.set_xlim(0, 3)
    ax.set_ylim(0, 3)

Thought: Colorbar already handles BoundaryNorm specially, avoiding calling inverse on it. In Artist.format_cursor_data, they call self.norm.inverse without checking. So fix should guard against non-invertible norms; either by try/except around self.norm.inverse, or by checking instance types. The easier safe fix: wrap inverse call in try/except ValueError and fall back to some alternative for delta calculation. We need to propose a complete fix: modify Artist.format_cursor_data to handle ValueError from norm.inverse and proceed with default behavior (use g_sig_digits with some fallback like 3?), or compute delta differently without using inverse.

Let's examine what format_cursor_data aims to compute: neighbors = self.norm.inverse((int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n); delta = abs(neighbors - data).max(); g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta) — they compute significant digits based on distance to midpoints of neighboring color intervals. For BoundaryNorm, perhaps neighbors should be boundaries of bin? For BoundaryNorm, self.norm maps to integers (0..Ncmap) — but inverse isn't implemented. For the purpose of formatting cursor data, simpler fallback: use default significant digits 3 when inverse fails. Tests earlier include case [[1, 1+1e-15]] expecting high sig digits; this relies on inverse available for Normalize (linear). For BoundaryNorm, probably 3 digits is okay.

What about other non-invertible norms? colorbar uses norm.inverse in multiple places, but already handled special-casing. Artist.format_cursor_data should perhaps check if hasattr(self.norm, 'inverse') and catch ValueError. Implement try/except ValueError around neighbors computation and set g_sig_digits=3 in except. But must also consider AttributeError or TypeError. Use try/except Exception? Better narrow to ValueError as BoundaryNorm raises ValueError. Could also check for isinstance(self.norm, colors.BoundaryNorm) — but importing colors here would cause circular? artist.py already imports matplotlib as mpl and from .cm import ScalarMappable; colors is mpl.colors maybe. We can import colors via mpl.colors. But more robust: try/except ValueError.

We must follow developer instruction: propose complete fix and list all files needing changes. Likely only artist.py changes. But check other places calling norm.inverse without handling — search earlier shows only artist.py and colorbar.py; colorbar already special-cases. So only artist.py needs change.

However, need to update tests possibly to add a test for the BoundaryNorm case; but here we only modify code. Provide code patch: in artist.py, change:

Root cause
- Artist.format_cursor_data unconditionally calls self.norm.inverse to compute the neighbor midpoints used to determine formatting precision. BoundaryNorm does not implement inverse (it raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible")), so the unguarded call raises ValueError during mouseover handling and crashes interaction.

- lib/matplotlib/artist.py

What the code currently does (in Artist.format_cursor_data):

                (int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)

- The only direct call to norm.inverse in the area that could be triggered during mouseover is in Artist.format_cursor_data; this is the site of the crash from the bug report. Other places that use norm.inverse (colorbar.py) already special-case BoundaryNorm or NoNorm. Therefore, correcting Artist.format_cursor_data covers the bug and does not leave other call sites unguarded.
- The fallback uses the same default digit count already present in the method for the normed==infinite case; this keeps behavior consistent.

Why not change BoundaryNorm.inverse to be "approximately invertible"?
- BoundaryNorm maps continuous values into discrete color indices (many-to-one). There is no unique inverse — any attempted inverse would be heuristic. Changing BoundaryNorm.inverse to return e.g. the bin edges for a given normalized index might have side effects elsewhere (code expects inverse to perform scaling from normalized [0,1] to data values), and colorbar.py already avoids calling inverse on BoundaryNorm. A defensive approach at the call site is safer and minimal.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

Summary of what I checked and why:
- I inspected the implementation of Artist.format_cursor_data in lib/matplotlib/artist.py to verify whether it calls norm.inverse unguarded and to confirm the surrounding logic (how g_sig_digits is chosen, and whether there is any existing fallback for non-invertible norms).
- I inspected BoundaryNorm.inverse in lib/matplotlib/colors.py to confirm that it intentionally raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible") and to capture the exact behavior and rationale.
- I inspected lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py to confirm whether other callers of norm.inverse handle BoundaryNorm specially (to validate the claim that the codebase already contains the defensive pattern).
- I searched for other occurrences of ".inverse(" usage in the codebase to determine whether any other callers share the same assumption (i.e., assume invertibility) and therefore should be included in the RootCause / impact.

Tool-by-tool rationale and results (already done prior to each tool call):
- search_codebase for "format_cursor_data" to locate the method and tests. Expected to find artist.py and tests. Result: artist.py contains format_cursor_data; tests reference it.
- get_file_context for lib/matplotlib/artist.py around format_cursor_data to read the exact implementation and confirm the call to norm.inverse. Expected to find unguarded call. Result: lines 1305–1313 include the unguarded call.
- search_codebase for "class BoundaryNorm" and get_file_context for lib/matplotlib/colors.py to read BoundaryNorm.inverse. Expected to find a ValueError raise. Result: BoundaryNorm.inverse raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible").
- search_codebase for "BoundaryNorm" and get_file_context for lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py to find how colorbar handles BoundaryNorm. Expected to see special-casing to avoid norm.inverse. Result: colorbar.py special-cases BoundaryNorm (uses _forward_boundaries/_inverse_boundaries or other branches) and avoids calling norm.inverse for BoundaryNorm in multiple places.
- search_codebase for ".inverse(" across lib/matplotlib to find other callers. Expected to find artist.py and colorbar.py; result: the primary callers are artist.py and colorbar.py (and method definitions in colors.py). colorbar.py calls inverse but does so behind BoundaryNorm checks.

Concrete code evidence (quotes and analysis)
1) Artist.format_cursor_data (lib/matplotlib/artist.py)
- Lines showing the unguarded call and surrounding logic:

  File lib/matplotlib/artist.py, lines 1301–1313:
  - n = self.cmap.N
  - if np.ma.getmask(data):
      return "[]"
  - normed = self.norm(data)
  - if np.isfinite(normed):
      # Midpoints of neighboring color intervals.
      neighbors = self.norm.inverse(
          (int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
      delta = abs(neighbors - data).max()
      g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta)
  - else:
      g_sig_digits = 3  # Consistent with default below.
  - return "[{:-#.{}g}]".format(data, g_sig_digits)

Analysis:
- The code computes normed = self.norm(data), then (when finite) calls self.norm.inverse(...) on a construct derived from self.norm(data). The call to self.norm.inverse is unguarded (no try/except), so any exception raised by inverse will propagate out of format_cursor_data.
- g_sig_digits is computed from cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta) where delta depends on neighbors returned by norm.inverse.

2) BoundaryNorm.inverse (lib/matplotlib/colors.py)
- Lines showing intentional exception:

  File lib/matplotlib/colors.py, lines 1904–1912:
  - def inverse(self, value):
      """
      Raises
      ------
      ValueError
          BoundaryNorm is not invertible, so calling this method will always
          raise an error
      """
      raise ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible")

Analysis:
- BoundaryNorm.inverse intentionally raises a ValueError: calling inverse on a BoundaryNorm will always raise this exception because BoundaryNorm maps continuous inputs to discrete bins (digitize), so there is no unique inverse.

3) colorbar.py special-casing (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py)
- Examples where colorbar avoids norm.inverse for BoundaryNorm:

  File lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py, lines 1236–1253:
  - if (self.boundaries is not None or isinstance(self.norm, colors.BoundaryNorm)):
      if self.spacing == 'uniform':
          funcs = (self._forward_boundaries, self._inverse_boundaries)
          self._set_scale('function', functions=funcs)
      elif self.spacing == 'proportional':
          self._set_scale('linear')
      ...
  - else:
      funcs = (self.norm, self.norm.inverse)
      self._set_scale('function', functions=funcs)

  File lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py, lines 1167–1169:
  - if (not isinstance(self.norm, colors.BoundaryNorm) and (self.boundaries is None)):
      b = self.norm.inverse(b)

  File lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py, lines 1188–1190:
  - if (isinstance(self.norm, (colors.BoundaryNorm, colors.NoNorm))
        or self.boundaries is not None):
      # not using a norm...

Analysis:
- colorbar.py contains explicit checks for BoundaryNorm and NoNorm and chooses alternative strategies (using explicit boundaries or forward/inverse-boundary functions) rather than calling norm.inverse directly. This shows that other parts of the codebase already recognize that BoundaryNorm is non-invertible and avoid calling inverse directly.

4) Search for other callers of ".inverse("
- Search results indicate the unguarded call in artist.py and several uses in colorbar.py. Most other ".inverse(" occurrences are definitions of inverse in Normalize subclasses or tests. No other callers in library modules were found that unguardedly call norm.inverse on arbitrary Norm instances.

Verification summary and mapping to the draft claims
- The draft enhanced report asserts:
  - Artist.format_cursor_data calls norm.inverse without guarding against exceptions — confirmed by the code excerpt above.
  - BoundaryNorm.inverse raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible") intentionally — confirmed.
  - colorbar.py special-cases BoundaryNorm/NoNorm and avoids calling norm.inverse — confirmed by multiple checks.
  - format_cursor_data has a fallback branch for non-finite normed values (g_sig_digits = 3) but no fallback for ValueError from norm.inverse — confirmed (there is an else: g_sig_digits = 3 only for non-finite normed values).
- All claims in the draft that I could check against source code are supported.

Completeness checks, other findings, and gaps
- The investigation suggested a minimal defensive fix (try/except ValueError) in Artist.format_cursor_data; that was a proposal in the investigation, not a claim about current code. I did not apply or include any patch — for diagnosis, it's appropriate to note the absence of defensive handling but not to prescribe a fix in the RootCause field.
- The investigation noted (and I verified) that colorbar.py already applies the correct defensive pattern — this supports the claim that a canonical pattern exists elsewhere in the codebase.
- Tests: The investigation mentioned that no unit test was added to assert that format_cursor_data does not raise for BoundaryNorm. I verified a tests file references format_cursor_data but did not find a test specifically covering BoundaryNorm in format_cursor_data. This is a gap in the investigation (no test existed or was added).
- Impact: Searching for other callers of norm.inverse did not reveal other unguarded uses aside from artist.py; colorbar.py has guarded uses. Therefore the scope of code that will raise the same user-visible exception (ValueError during mouseover) is localized to Artist.format_cursor_data (when the artist is a ScalarMappable/Image using a BoundaryNorm) — this matches the original bug reproduction and the draft.

Field discipline and drafting decisions I will apply to the revised report
- Description: keep concise and factual (what happens).
- RootCause: restrict to incorrect assumptions and locations that encode them. Remove any prescriptive or "how to fix" language. For each finding include the assumption, the location, and the concrete behavior (e.g., that BoundaryNorm.inverse raises ValueError).
- StepsToReproduce / ExpectedBehavior / ObservedBehavior: keep user-visible statements only.
- Ensure that all secondary findings that share the same root cause pattern (i.e., the presence of other callers and code that already defends against non-invertible norms) are included in RootCause with equal weight.

bug_report.Title: Artist.format_cursor_data crashes when using BoundaryNorm (ValueError: BoundaryNorm is not invertible)
bug_report.Description: When an AxesImage/ScalarMappable is drawn with a BoundaryNorm, hovering (cursor/mouseover) causes Artist.format_cursor_data to call norm.inverse and raise ValueError, which stops interactive mouseover handling.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/artist.py:Artist.format_cursor_data assumes that self.norm is invertible and calls self.norm.inverse(...) without guarding against exceptions. Concretely, when np.isfinite(self.norm(data)) is true it performs:
   neighbors = self.norm.inverse((int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
   and then uses neighbors to compute delta and g_sig_digits. Because the inverse() call is unguarded, any exception raised by a Norm.inverse implementation will propagate out of format_cursor_data.

2) lib/matplotlib/colors.py:BoundaryNorm.inverse intentionally raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible"). BoundaryNorm maps continuous values into discrete bins (via digitize) and therefore has no unique inverse; calling inverse on a BoundaryNorm always raises this ValueError. This is the exact exception seen in the reported stack trace.

3) lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py contains multiple callers that recognize non-invertible norms and avoid calling norm.inverse (for example, by using self._boundaries or (_forward_boundaries, _inverse_boundaries) when isinstance(self.norm, colors.BoundaryNorm) or colors.NoNorm). These locations include, among others, _process_values, _reset_locator_formatter_scale, and _locate. The presence of these special-cases shows the canonical defensive pattern elsewhere in the codebase, and highlights the inconsistency that Artist.format_cursor_data does not follow the same defensive approach.

4) format_cursor_data does have a fallback for the case where the normalized value is not finite (it sets g_sig_digits = 3), but it has no fallback for a ValueError raised by norm.inverse; therefore users will see an exception when hovering over images with non-invertible norms such as BoundaryNorm.
StepsToReproduce: Create a script with the following content:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
norm = mpl.colors.BoundaryNorm(np.linspace(-4, 4, 5), 256)
X = np.random.randn(10, 10)
pc = ax.imshow(X, cmap='RdBu_r', norm=norm)
Show the figure and move the mouse cursor over the image (mouseover).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Mouseover over the image should not raise an exception or stop interactive handling; the cursor tooltip/formatting should remain functional (displaying formatted data) and interaction should continue.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Hovering the image raises ValueError: "BoundaryNorm is not invertible" (originating from BoundaryNorm.inverse called by Artist.format_cursor_data), which interrupts mouseover interaction and causes the GUI handler to fail.

- What I learned from _pylab_helpers.py:
  - Gcf is a singleton-like class that stores an OrderedDict mapping figure numbers to managers in the class attribute figs.
  - The only places that clear or remove entries are:
    - set_active (adds/updates and moves to end)
    - destroy (removes manager.num from figs then manager.destroy())
    - destroy_fig (finds manager with matching figure and calls destroy)
    - destroy_all (disconnects and destroys all managers then cls.figs.clear())
  - So if Gcf.figs becomes empty, this comes from destroy or destroy_all (or direct .clear()).
  - Relevant methods: destroy_all, destroy, destroy_fig.

- Next: search for get_backend / rc_context and the code that reads rcParams['backend'], since calling matplotlib.get_backend() was the triggering action in the report.

- What I learned from reading matplotlib/__init__.py:
  - get_backend() returns rcParams['backend'] (line ~1170).
  - rc_context is implemented as a contextmanager that copies rcParams at entry (orig = rcParams.copy()), optionally updates rcParams (rc_file or rc update) during the context, yields, and in finally does dict.update(rcParams, orig) — unconditionally restoring all rcParams to orig.
  - RcParams.__getitem__ has special-case behavior:
    - If key == "backend" and self is the global rcParams, then it will read the value and if it is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel, import matplotlib.pyplot as plt and call plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).
    - Note: _get_backend_or_none exists to read backend without triggering resolution.
  - rcParamsDefault is set with backend default being rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel if the default rc has no 'backend' entry.
  - switch_backend in pyplot (called via plt.switch_backend) begins by calling close("all") (pyplot.switch_backend calls close('all') before loading new backend).

- Analysis of repro with these pieces:
  - A figure created inside rc_context can cause the backend to be resolved/loaded (because pyplot imports the backend to create a FigureManager).
  - rc_context on exit unconditionally restores the saved-original rcParams (orig). If orig had 'backend' set to the auto sentinel (which is common if the RC file contained no explicit backend), then dict.update(rcParams, orig) will set rcParams['backend'] back to the sentinel even though a backend module was loaded during the context and figures exist.
  - Later, calling matplotlib.get_backend() performs rcParams['backend'] (on the global rcParams) — this triggers RcParams.__getitem__ special-case: since value is sentinel, it will import pyplot and call plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).
  - switch_backend(auto) (the auto-resolve path) calls close("all") and then proceeds to determine a backend and set things up. The close("all") will call Gcf.destroy_all() which clears Gcf.figs — thus all figures are removed.
  - The net result: a figure created inside rc_context survives creation, but after rc_context exit rcParams['backend'] is reset to sentinel; then get_backend() triggers re-resolution of backend and unconditionally closes all figures, emptying Gcf.figs. This is exactly the bug observed: calling_get_backend() removed the figure(s), when the first figure in Gcf.figs was created in rc_context.

- Clear statement of the bug and the primary responsible code:
  - Bug: rc_context's unconditional restoration of rcParams (including the backend value) can reset rcParams['backend'] to the auto-backend sentinel. Later calls that access rcParams['backend'] (e.g., matplotlib.get_backend()) trigger backend resolution via pyplot.switch_backend when the sentinel is present; switch_backend closes all figures (close("all")), which empties Gcf.figs and effectively loses figure managers created earlier. This happens when a backend was implicitly loaded inside rc_context (e.g., by creating a figure), and the saved original rcParams had backend set to the auto sentinel.
  - Primary responsible location: the unconditional revert in rc_context (matplotlib.__init__.py, function rc_context), together with RcParams.__getitem__ special-case that triggers switch_backend for the auto-backend sentinel. The root offender is rc_context unconditionally reverting backend to the sentinel when it should not overwrite a resolved backend that was selected during the context.
