def test_array_patch_perimeters():
    # This compares the old implementation as a reference for the
    # vectorized one.
    def check(x, rstride, cstride):
        rows, cols = x.shape
        row_inds = [*range(0, rows-1, rstride), rows-1]
        col_inds = [*range(0, cols-1, cstride), cols-1]
        polys = []
        for rs, rs_next in zip(row_inds[:-1], row_inds[1:]):
            for cs, cs_next in zip(col_inds[:-1], col_inds[1:]):
                # +1 ensures we share edges between polygons
                ps = cbook._array_perimeter(x[rs:rs_next+1, cs:cs_next+1]).T
                polys.append(ps)
        polys = np.asarray(polys)
        assert np.array_equal(polys,
                              cbook._array_patch_perimeters(
                                  x, rstride=rstride, cstride=cstride))

    def divisors(n):
        return [i for i in range(1, n + 1) if n % i == 0]

    for rows, cols in [(5, 5), (7, 14), (13, 9)]:
        x = np.arange(rows * cols).reshape(rows, cols)
        for rstride, cstride in itertools.product(divisors(rows - 1),
                                                  divisors(cols - 1)):
            check(x, rstride=rstride, cstride=cstride)


def test_setattr_cm():
    class A:
        cls_level = object()
        override = object()

        def __init__(self):
            self.aardvark = 'aardvark'
            self.override = 'override'
            self._p = 'p'

        def meth(self):
            ...

        @classmethod
        def classy(cls):
            ...

        @staticmethod
        def static():
            ...

        @property
        def prop(self):
            return self._p

        @prop.setter
        def prop(self, val):
            self._p = val

    class B(A):
        ...

    other = A()

    def verify_pre_post_state(obj):
        # When you access a Python method the function is bound
        # to the object at access time so you get a new instance
        # of MethodType every time.
        #
        # https://docs.python.org/3/howto/descriptor.html#functions-and-methods
        assert obj.meth is not obj.meth
        # normal attribute should give you back the same instance every time
        assert obj.aardvark is obj.aardvark
        assert a.aardvark == 'aardvark'
        # and our property happens to give the same instance every time
        assert obj.prop is obj.prop
        assert obj.cls_level is A.cls_level
        assert obj.override == 'override'
        assert not hasattr(obj, 'extra')
        assert obj.prop == 'p'
        assert obj.monkey == other.meth
        assert obj.cls_level is A.cls_level
        assert 'cls_level' not in obj.__dict__
        assert 'classy' not in obj.__dict__
        assert 'static' not in obj.__dict__

    a = B()

    a.monkey = other.meth
    verify_pre_post_state(a)
    with cbook._setattr_cm(
            a, prop='squirrel',
            aardvark='moose', meth=lambda: None,
            override='boo', extra='extra',
            monkey=lambda: None, cls_level='bob',
            classy='classy', static='static'):
        # because we have set a lambda, it is normal attribute access
        # and the same every time
        assert a.meth is a.meth
        assert a.aardvark is a.aardvark
        assert a.aardvark == 'moose'
        assert a.override == 'boo'
        assert a.extra == 'extra'
        assert a.prop == 'squirrel'
        assert a.monkey != other.meth
        assert a.cls_level == 'bob'
        assert a.classy == 'classy'
        assert a.static == 'static'

    verify_pre_post_state(a)

@_docstring.dedent_interpd
def table(ax,
          cellText=None, cellColours=None,
          cellLoc='right', colWidths=None,
          rowLabels=None, rowColours=None, rowLoc='left',
          colLabels=None, colColours=None, colLoc='center',
          loc='bottom', bbox=None, edges='closed',
          **kwargs):
    """
    Add a table to an `~.axes.Axes`.

    At least one of *cellText* or *cellColours* must be specified. These
    parameters must be 2D lists, in which the outer lists define the rows and
    the inner list define the column values per row. Each row must have the
    same number of elements.

    The table can optionally have row and column headers, which are configured
    using *rowLabels*, *rowColours*, *rowLoc* and *colLabels*, *colColours*,
    *colLoc* respectively.

    For finer grained control over tables, use the `.Table` class and add it to
    the axes with `.Axes.add_table`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    cellText : 2D list of str, optional
        The texts to place into the table cells.

        *Note*: Line breaks in the strings are currently not accounted for and
        will result in the text exceeding the cell boundaries.

    cellColours : 2D list of colors, optional
        The background colors of the cells.

    cellLoc : {'left', 'center', 'right'}, default: 'right'
        The alignment of the text within the cells.

    colWidths : list of float, optional
        The column widths in units of the axes. If not given, all columns will
        have a width of *1 / ncols*.

    rowLabels : list of str, optional
        The text of the row header cells.

def update_datalim(self, xys, updatex=True, updatey=True):
        """
        Extend the `~.Axes.dataLim` Bbox to include the given points.

        If no data is set currently, the Bbox will ignore its limits and set
        the bound to be the bounds of the xydata (*xys*). Otherwise, it will
        compute the bounds of the union of its current data and the data in
        *xys*.

        Parameters
        ----------
        xys : 2D array-like
            The points to include in the data limits Bbox. This can be either
            a list of (x, y) tuples or a Nx2 array.

        updatex, updatey : bool, default: True
            Whether to update the x/y limits.
        """
        xys = np.asarray(xys)
        if not np.any(np.isfinite(xys)):
            return
        self.dataLim.update_from_data_xy(xys, self.ignore_existing_data_limits,
                                         updatex=updatex, updatey=updatey)
        self.ignore_existing_data_limits = False

    def _process_unit_info(self, datasets=None, kwargs=None, *, convert=True):
        """
        Set axis units based on *datasets* and *kwargs*, and optionally apply
        unit conversions to *datasets*.

        Parameters
        ----------
        datasets : list
            List of (axis_name, dataset) pairs (where the axis name is defined
            as in `._axis_map`).  Individual datasets can also be None
            (which gets passed through).
        kwargs : dict
            Other parameters from which unit info (i.e., the *xunits*,
            *yunits*, *zunits* (for 3D Axes), *runits* and *thetaunits* (for
            polar) entries) is popped, if present.  Note that this dict is
            mutated in-place!
        convert : bool, default: True
            Whether to return the original datasets or the converted ones.

        Returns
        -------
        list
            Either the original datasets if *convert* is False, or the
            converted ones if *convert* is True (the default).
        """
        # The API makes datasets a list of pairs rather than an axis_name to
        # dataset mapping because it is sometimes necessary to process multiple
        # datasets for a single axis, and concatenating them may be tricky
        # (e.g. if some are scalars, etc.).
        datasets = datasets or []
        kwargs = kwargs or {}
        axis_map = self._axis_map
        for axis_name, data in datasets:
            try:
                axis = axis_map[axis_name]
            except KeyError:
                raise ValueError(f"Invalid axis name: {axis_name!r}") from None
            # Update from data if axis is already set but no unit is set yet.
            if axis is not None and data is not None and not axis.have_units():
                axis.update_units(data)
        for axis_name, axis in axis_map.items():
            # Return if no axis is set.
            if axis is None:
                continue
            # Check for units in the kwargs, and if present update axis.
            units = kwargs.pop(f"{axis_name}units", axis.units)
            if self.name == "polar":
                # Special case: polar supports "thetaunits"/"runits".
                polar_units = {"x": "thetaunits", "y": "runits"}
                units = kwargs.pop(polar_units[axis_name], units)
            if units != axis.units and units is not None:
                axis.set_units(units)
                # If the units being set imply a different converter,
                # we need to update again.
                for dataset_axis_name, data in datasets:
                    if dataset_axis_name == axis_name and data is not None:
                        axis.update_units(data)
        return [axis_map[axis_name].convert_units(data)
                if convert and data is not None else data
                for axis_name, data in datasets]

        """

        """
        return self.patch.contains(mouseevent)[0]

Findings in Axes._axes.py:
- Axes._convert_dx (staticmethod) tries to find a "first finite" element in x0 and xconv via cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv).
- The call is inside a try/except; however the inner try/except that wraps cbook._safe_first_finite only catches (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) — it does not catch StopIteration.
- If cbook._safe_first_finite raises StopIteration (which it does when there is no finite element in the iterable), it propagates up and causes the exception observed.
- The outer try/except only catches (ValueError, TypeError, AttributeError) around the arithmetic and conversion logic — that does not catch StopIteration.

Tool call: get_file_context lib/matplotlib/cbook/__init__.py around _safe_first_finite
- What I need: inspect _safe_first_finite to see what it does and what exceptions it raises.

Findings in cbook._safe_first_finite:
- Definition: _safe_first_finite(obj, *, skip_nonfinite=True)
- It defines safe_isfinite(val) that returns False for None; for scalar uses numpy.isfinite; for non-scalar returns True (i.e., non-scalar objects are treated as "finite").
- Behavior:
  - If skip_nonfinite is False: it returns next(iter(obj)) or obj[0] for iterators (with a RuntimeError on generator).
  - Else:
    - If obj is np.flatiter: return obj[0]
    - elif obj is an iterator: raise RuntimeError("matplotlib does not support generators as input")
    - else: return next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val))
- Note: next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val)) raises StopIteration if the generator is exhausted (i.e., if there are no finite elements).
- So _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration when skip_nonfinite=True and there are no finite elements.

Conclusion for Checkpoint 1:
- The exception arises because Axes._convert_dx calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) when x0 is [np.nan]. _safe_first_finite, looking for a finite element, finds none and raises StopIteration. Axes._convert_dx does not catch StopIteration at that call site, so StopIteration propagates and the bar call crashes.
- The primary responsible method is Axes._convert_dx (in lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py). cbook._safe_first_finite behavior is correct for its contract, but callers need to handle the StopIteration case when the iterable contains no finite values.

Proposed fix (high level)
- The root cause is an uncaught StopIteration from cbook._safe_first_finite inside Axes._convert_dx when the x-values contain only non-finite values (NaN).   2) In the errorbar helper _upcast_err: protect the calls to cbook._safe_first_finite(err) with try/except that catches StopIteration, falling back to the existing "non-array" path.
  3) In _parse_scatter_color_args: the expression using cbook._safe_first_finite(c) to decide whether c is string-like should be made robust to StopIteration.

- Axes._convert_dx is the direct site producing the reported StopIteration in the trace — first priority.
- _upcast_err uses _safe_first_finite similarly to detect array-like inner types; if _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration, we should fall back to the branch that wraps err as dtype=object. Otherwise a user passing all-NaN xerr/xerr could cause StopIteration in errorbar.

Now I will answer the required challenge questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence answer: The existing code assumes that when it calls cbook._safe_first_finite on an iterable there will be at least one finite element (so _safe_first_finite won't raise StopIteration); the fix removes that assumption by treating a StopIteration (no finite elements present) as a normal fallback case rather than an uncaught exception.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- I inspected cbook._safe_first_finite and the call sites in Axes. There are no numeric "hardcoded" constants encoding the assumption that an iterable contains a finite element. The assumption is implicit in the call sites not catching StopIteration. The only "magic" behavior was that safe_isfinite returns True for non-scalar items (so tuples will be accepted as "finite"), which is a deliberate design, not a numeric constant to change.
- Conclusion: There are no numeric hardcoded values that must become dynamic for this fix. What needed to change is catching StopIteration at call sites.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse (complementary) operations here are the parent class's methods that operate on / read the results (adding patches, updating data limits, autoscaling). I re-examined the relevant inherited parent methods to ensure they behave correctly when bar creates patches with NaN positions:
  - _AxesBase.add_patch calls _update_patch_limits which collects vertices and transforms them; update_datalim ignores points that are not finite: update_datalim begins with "if not np.any(np.isfinite(xys)): return" — so it will not update data limits if all coordinates are non-finite. This prevents autoscaling from being corrupted by NaNs.
  - autoscale_view handles sticky edges, locator.nonsingular, etc. It expects data limits to be finite or else uses locator.nonsingular to produce reasonable defaults. If no finite points were added, autoscale_view will not expand view limits incorrectly.
- Concrete substitution trace for parent autoscale/update logic:
  - Variables of interest: patch vertices for a Rectangle placed at (nan, nan) (left/top) and width/height nan — _update_patch_limits gathers vertices, transforms them, and passes them to self.update_datalim. If those transformed vertices are all non-finite, update_datalim returns early and does not change data limits, consistent with previous behavior (3.6.0 returned Rectangle with NaN coords but did not change datalim).
  - Therefore, the reverse operation (autoscaling / update_datalim) handles NaN coordinates by ignoring them; no parent overrides need to be changed.
- Conclusion: No complementary parent methods need to be overridden as a result of this change.

- I considered Axes.bar and the other affected methods that call _convert_dx:

With the change catching StopIteration, in the all-NaN-x case the conversion will fall back to the outer except path which converts dx by itself (same as before for other failing arithmetic), and width will be set. Bar will continue to create Rectangle patches with NaN coordinates (matching the 3.6.0 expected behavior).
  - errorbar._upcast_err: By guarding _safe_first_finite(err) with try/except StopIteration, we avoid raising StopIteration for all-NaN err arrays and instead fall back to object-array path. This is safer and consistent with the existing fallback behavior, so errorbar remains functional.
  - scatter._parse_scatter_color_args: By catching StopIteration when checking the first element of c, we avoid raising and treat c as not string-like; scatter's existing fallback branches remain intact.
- No other Axes methods were changed. The small defensive changes avoid previously unhandled StopIteration. All existing methods should keep working; in particular, autoscaling, update_datalim, and patch handling were left unchanged and are robust to NaN.

- I reviewed the parent _AxesBase methods which could be related: update_datalim, add_patch, autoscale_view, etc. I re-read those implementations where necessary (see above). None of them need to be overridden. They already handle NaN values in a safe way (e.g., update_datalim checks np.isfinite).
- Therefore, no new methods are required.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and patch locations)

Root cause (concise):
- Axes._convert_dx calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) and did not catch StopIteration. When the x positions are all NaN (no finite elements), _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration; that exception was not handled and bubbled up, causing ax.bar([...nan...], [...nan...]) to raise StopIteration. The code probably intended to treat "no finite item found" as "fallback to convert dx by itself" (which is done in the outer arithmetic fallback), but forgot to catch StopIteration.

Every location that needs change (and what to change)
1) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py — Axes._convert_dx
   - Current behavior:
     - Calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) inside a try/except that catches (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) but not StopIteration. Similarly for xconv.
     - If StopIteration occurs, it propagates and crashes calls like ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan]).
   - Fix:
     - Add StopIteration to the except tuples so that StopIteration is handled the same as TypeError/IndexError/KeyError, i.e. go to fallback logic instead of letting StopIteration escape.
   - Code change (two locations inside _convert_dx):
     - except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError):
       -> except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError, StopIteration):
     - and similarly for xconv handling.

2) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py — Axes.errorbar helper _upcast_err
   - Current behavior:
     - Uses cbook._safe_first_finite(err) multiple times without catching StopIteration; if err is an iterable with no finite elements, _safe_first_finite will raise StopIteration and errorbar will crash.
   - Fix:
     - Call _safe_first_finite(err) once inside a try/except catching (TypeError, StopIteration). If it raises, treat as ff = None and fall back to the existing "wrap in object array" behavior.
   - Concrete modification: cache the first finite element in a variable `ff` and test isinstance(ff, np.ndarray) instead of calling _safe_first_finite repeatedly.

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py — Axes._parse_scatter_color_args
   - Current behavior:
     - Uses cbook._safe_first_finite(c) inline to detect if the first element is a string; if c is an iterable of numeric nan values then _safe_first_finite will raise StopIteration and scatter will crash.
   - Fix:
     - Replace the inline call with a try/except protecting against StopIteration (and TypeError) and set c_is_string_or_strings accordingly.
   - Concrete modification: try to get firstc = cbook._safe_first_finite(c); if it raises StopIteration or TypeError, set firstc=None and treat as not string-like.

Why these changes are sufficient (and minimal)
- The immediate crash came from an uncaught StopIteration in Axes._convert_dx — adding StopIteration to the specific except clauses fixes the direct symptom.
- Other call sites in Axes that do similar probing with _safe_first_finite, but didn't guard StopIteration, could also fail in edge cases (xerr, colors). I updated the ones that are likely to be hit by user code (errorbar, scatter), preventing similar crashes.
- I avoided changing the semantics of cbook._safe_first_finite itself because it has a specific contract (it uses generator expression 'next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val))' which naturally raises StopIteration when no such element exists), and other code expects StopIteration in some places (some call sites already catch it). Changing the central function would be broader and risk breaking other call sites/tests.

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic:
- None. This bug was not due to numeric constants; it was due to an uncaught StopIteration. No hardcoded numbers need to be changed.

Reverse operation (reading) analysis:
- The change affects writes (construction of Rectangle with potentially NaN x). The complement (reading or autoscaling) is done by inherited _AxesBase.update_datalim and autoscale_view, which already guard against NaN values (update_datalim returns early when no finite points are present). I traced the code paths (bar -> add_patch -> _update_patch_limits -> update_datalim -> autoscale_view) and found no action required; they handle NaNs gracefully.
- I also verified there is no parent method performing the exact reverse arithmetic that would be invalidated by the change.

Does any new method need to be added?
- No.

compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: ax.bar([...np.nan...], [...np.nan...]) raises StopIteration because Axes calls cbook._safe_first_finite on the x iterable and does not handle the case where there is no finite element. The wrong assumption: callers assume _safe_first_finite will always return a value.
- Root cause location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py — Axes._convert_dx calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) but does NOT catch StopIteration (it only catches TypeError/IndexError/KeyError). The StopIteration from _safe_first_finite is therefore allowed to bubble up.
- Proposed fix: add StopIteration to the existing except clauses at the call sites in Axes that probe the "first finite" element, and make a couple of other Axes call sites defensive (cache the first-element lookup inside try/except StopIteration). Primary edits: Axes._convert_dx, the _upcast_err helper in errorbar, and the first-element check in _parse_scatter_color_args (scatter). This is a minimal, caller-side defensive fix — do not change cbook._safe_first_finite.
- Hardcoded values/constants: none. The bug is an uncaught exception, not a numeric constant; no numeric hardcodes need to become dynamic.
- Methods to add/override: none. Parent class behavior (add_patch/update_datalim/autoscale) already tolerates NaNs; no new methods are required.
- Gaps remaining: the investigator did not (a) run the test-suite or apply the patch in a runtime to verify end-to-end behavior, and (b) execute a concrete trace by creating a Rectangle with NaN coords and observing update_datalim/autoscale behavior at runtime (they inspected code but did not execute). Also did not exhaustively audit all non-Axes callers of _safe_first_finite outside the repo subset they searched.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: StopIteration raised by cbook._safe_first_finite (Checkpoint 1)
What: lib/matplotlib/cbook/__init__.py::_safe_first_finite(obj, *, skip_nonfinite=True) uses next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val)). If no finite value exists, that generator raises StopIteration. This is the direct exception seen in the traceback and is expected given the function's implementation. It matters because callers must treat "no finite element" as a normal fallback condition, not a crash.

### Finding 2: Axes._convert_dx does not catch StopIteration (Checkpoint 1 / 4)
What: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._convert_dx(dx, x0, xconv, convert) calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) inside except blocks that catch (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) but NOT StopIteration. Because _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration when there are no finite items (e.g., x0 == [np.nan]), StopIteration escapes and breaks ax.bar. This is the immediate root cause seen in the bug report.

### Finding 3: Other Axes probes also call _safe_first_finite without catching StopIteration (Checkpoint 4)
What: The Axes implementation uses _safe_first_finite elsewhere to probe the first element's type (notably the _upcast_err helper inside errorbar and the first-element check in _parse_scatter_color_args). These sites currently call _safe_first_finite inline and would also raise StopIteration for all-nonfinite iterables; they need the same defensive treatment (try/catch and fall back).

### Finding 4: Parent methods already tolerate NaN (Checkpoint 3c)
What: The inherited path (bar -> add_patch -> _update_patch_limits -> update_datalim -> autoscale_view) is already robust: update_datalim and related code check np.isfinite and return/ignore non-finite coordinates. Thus allowing Bars/Rectangles with NaN x/height is safe and restores 3.6.0 behavior; no parent-method change is required. The agent inspected these code paths but did not run a concrete runtime test that exercises a Rectangle with NaN coordinates.

### Finding 5: Fix strategy scope and residual risks (Checkpoint 4 / 5)
What: Minimal, caller-side fixes (catch StopIteration at Axes call sites) are safer than changing cbook._safe_first_finite globally. However, the investigator did not run tests; other callers of _safe_first_finite outside the inspected list may also require similar handling. This is a remaining investigation gap.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._convert_dx
- Wrong assumption: The code assumes cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) / cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) will not raise StopIteration (i.e., that an iterable will contain at least one finite element).
- Evidence: Current code catches only (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) around calls to cbook._safe_first_finite; the traceback in the bug report shows StopIteration originates inside cbook._safe_first_finite and propagates to Axes._convert_dx. Exact relevant code fragments:
  - existing excepts (examples): except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError):  # DOES NOT include StopIteration
  - proposed minimal change: add StopIteration to the tuple:
    - except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError, StopIteration):
- Related locations: other callers in Axes that call _safe_first_finite (see below). Fixing this one location addresses the crash shown in the bug trace.

### Location: lib/matplotlib/cbook/__init__.py:_safe_first_finite
- Wrong assumption (in callers): Callers implicitly assumed this helper returns a value rather than letting StopIteration escape; _safe_first_finite's behavior (generator next(...)) will raise StopIteration if no element passes safe_isfinite.
- Evidence: Implementation excerpt:
  - def _safe_first_finite(obj, *, skip_nonfinite=True):
      ...
      return next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val))
    This generator raises StopIteration if the iterable has no safe_isfinite(val) True elements (e.g., [np.nan]).
- Related locations: All call sites of _safe_first_finite (search results: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py, lib/matplotlib/dates.py, lib/matplotlib/units.py, tests). Some call sites already catch StopIteration (e.g., dates.default_units wraps in try/except StopIteration), others do not.

### Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._upcast_err (helper inside errorbar)
- Wrong assumption: The helper calls cbook._safe_first_finite(err) repeatedly and assumes it won't raise StopIteration.
- Evidence: Code uses isinstance(cbook._safe_first_finite(err), np.ndarray) and then type(cbook._safe_first_finite(err)) without guarding for StopIteration. If err is an iterable of only NaNs, _safe_first_finite(err) raises StopIteration and errorbar would crash.
- Concrete fix pattern:
  - ff = None
    try:
        ff = cbook._safe_first_finite(err)
    except (TypeError, StopIteration):
        ff = None
    if np.iterable(err) and len(err) > 0 and isinstance(ff, np.ndarray):
        atype = type(ff)
        ...
- Related locations: other code paths that probe error arrays (errorbar internals).

### Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._parse_scatter_color_args (scatter)
- Wrong assumption: Inline use of cbook._safe_first_finite(c) assumes it returns a first element; it does not handle StopIteration.
- Evidence: existing check pattern:
  - c_is_string_or_strings = (
      isinstance(c, str)
      or (np.iterable(c) and len(c) > 0
          and isinstance(cbook._safe_first_finite(c), str)))
  If c is an iterable whose only elements are non-finite (NaN), _safe_first_finite(c) raises StopIteration and scatter will raise.
- Concrete fix pattern:
  - c_is_string_or_strings = isinstance(c, str)
    if not c_is_string_or_strings and np.iterable(c) and len(c) > 0:
        try:
            firstc = cbook._safe_first_finite(c)
        except (TypeError, StopIteration):
            firstc = None
        c_is_string_or_strings = isinstance(firstc, str)
- Related locations: any Axes routines that detect sequence element types via _safe_first_finite (search results in this repo show a small set; adjust similarly).

### Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py:_update_patch_limits and update_datalim (evidence for reverse operation)
- Wrong assumption addressed: None (these methods already assume NaN coordinates may appear and are defensive).
- Evidence: update_datalim and related logic explicitly check np.isfinite on coordinate arrays and return/ignore non-finite values, which preserves sensible autoscale behavior when patches with NaN coordinates are added. This is why letting Axes create Rectangle(s) with NaN x/height is acceptable.
- Related locations: Axes.add_patch -> _update_patch_limits -> update_datalim -> autoscale_view. No changes needed here, but a concrete runtime test with a Rectangle with NaN coords was not executed by the investigator (gap).

Notes on gaps (evidence locker):
- The investigator did not run unit tests or runtime checks after proposed edits (risk: other callers of _safe_first_finite outside the audited list may require similar defensiveness).
- The investigator inspected parent autoscale/update paths in code (found them tolerant of NaNs) but did not execute a live trace creating a Rectangle with NaN coordinates to confirm no side-effect on axes limits.

Would you like the exact patch/diff for the three code edits (ready to apply), or prefer that I also search for every other call site of _safe_first_finite across the entire repository and produce a patch bundle?

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft enhanced report against the source code and the compressed investigation analysis. For each code location I accessed I explain why and what I expected to learn before the call; after each retrieval I analyze what the code shows and how it supports or contradicts the draft.

1) Verify cbook._safe_first_finite (why I fetched it)
- Reason to fetch: the draft attributes the StopIteration to cbook._safe_first_finite using next(...). I expected to confirm the implementation and whether it raises StopIteration for iterables with no finite elements.
- What I looked for: the code path that returns the "first finite" value and whether it uses next(...) over a generator.

Result (from lib/matplotlib/cbook/__init__.py lines ~1749):
- Implementation excerpt: "return next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val))"
- Analysis: This directly uses next(...) over a generator expression and therefore will raise StopIteration when no element in obj passes safe_isfinite — for example when obj == [np.nan]. This confirms the draft's statement that _safe_first_finite propagates StopIteration for all-nonfinite input.

2) Verify Axes._convert_dx (why I fetched it)
- Reason to fetch: the traceback in the original report implicates Axes._convert_dx; the draft claims _convert_dx calls _safe_first_finite and does not catch StopIteration.
- What I expected: see the try/except blocks around _safe_first_finite calls and which exception types are caught.

Result (from lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py lines ~2170-2199):
- Code shows:
  - try:
      x0 = cbook._safe_first_finite(x0)
    except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError):
      pass
  - try:
      x = cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv)
    except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError):
      x = xconv
- Analysis: These except clauses do not include StopIteration. Because _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration on all-nonfinite iterables, StopIteration will not be caught here and will therefore escape from _convert_dx, matching the observed traceback and the draft claim. This confirms the direct root cause for ax.bar failing when the x iterable contains only NaNs.

3) Verify other Axes call sites mentioned (why I fetched them)
- Reason to fetch: the draft lists other Axes helpers (errorbar internals, scatter color parsing, broken_barh) that call _safe_first_finite without guarding StopIteration. I expected to confirm those usages and whether they catch StopIteration or not.

Results:
- Axes._upcast_err (lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py lines ~3454-3492):
  - The helper uses isinstance(cbook._safe_first_finite(err), np.ndarray) and then type(cbook._safe_first_finite(err)) without try/except guarding StopIteration.
  - Analysis: This is an unguarded call; if err is an iterable with no finite elements, _safe_first_finite will raise StopIteration here and thus errorbar-related code could raise StopIteration unexpectedly. This confirms the draft's claim about _upcast_err.

- Axes._parse_scatter_color_args (lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py lines ~4359-4362):
  - It computes c_is_string_or_strings as:
      isinstance(c, str) or (np.iterable(c) and len(c) > 0 and isinstance(cbook._safe_first_finite(c), str))
  - Analysis: There is no try/except around _safe_first_finite here. If c is an iterable whose elements are all non-finite in the sense used by safe_isfinite (note: safe_isfinite is used for numeric finiteness testing; here it is being used to probe the "first element" semantics), _safe_first_finite can raise StopIteration. This matches the draft's claim that scatter color parsing can raise StopIteration in this situation.

- Axes.broken_barh (lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py lines ~2830-2836):
  - Code uses:
      if len(xranges):
          xdata = cbook._safe_first_finite(xranges)
      ...
      if len(yrange):
          ydata = cbook._safe_first_finite(yrange)
  - Analysis: These calls are not guarded by try/except StopIteration. They are additional Axes call sites where the same root cause can arise for all-nonfinite inputs.

4) Verify autoscale/update_datalim and patch update behavior (why I fetched them)
- Reason to fetch: the draft asserts that downstream autoscaling and patch-limit update code tolerates NaNs (i.e., it's safe to create Rectangle(s) with NaN coordinates).
- What I expected: to find early-return or finite checks in update_datalim or related functions.

Result (from lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py lines ~2521-2543 and ~2420-2460):
- update_datalim contains:
    xys = np.asarray(xys)
    if not np.any(np.isfinite(xys)):
        return
  which returns early if there are no finite values.
- _update_patch_limits collects vertices and calls self.update_datalim(xys, ...)
- Analysis: update_datalim explicitly ignores/update nothing when the provided xy array contains no finite elements; _update_patch_limits transforms patch vertices and calls update_datalim. This supports the draft's observation that the parent autoscale/datalim path already tolerates NaNs, meaning that creating patches/rectangles with NaN coords is safe (i.e., downstream code won't crash due to those NaNs).

5) Search for all call sites of _safe_first_finite (why I searched)
- Reason to fetch: to ensure completeness — all call sites that share the same pattern (probing a first finite element and not handling StopIteration) must be represented in RootCause.
- What I expected: to find usages in axes, dates, units, tests, etc.

Result (repo-wide search):
- Occurrences found include:
  - lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py (several locations already inspected: _convert_dx, broken_barh, _upcast_err, _parse_scatter_color_args)
  - lib/matplotlib/dates.py: default_units wraps a call to _safe_first_finite inside try/except (lines ~1862-1865)
  - lib/matplotlib/units.py: Registry.get_converter uses _safe_first_finite inside a try/except that includes StopIteration (lines ~200-202)
  - lib/matplotlib/tests/test_cbook.py: tests call _safe_first_finite (several test locations)
- Analysis: There are multiple call sites across the codebase. Importantly, some call sites already handle StopIteration (dates.default_units, units.Registry.get_converter), demonstrating that the codebase contains examples of defensive handling; other Axes call sites do not, and those are where StopIteration can bubble up and cause the observed ax.bar failure.

6) Cross-check the draft's RootCause and investigator findings against code evidence

bug_report.Title: ax.bar raises StopIteration for all-NaN x inputs (matplotlib 3.6.1)
bug_report.Description: Calling Axes.bar (and some other Axes methods) with x (and/or related) sequences that contain only non-finite values (e.g. [np.nan]) can raise StopIteration. The exception originates because callers probe the "first finite" element via cbook._safe_first_finite, which raises StopIteration when no finite element exists, and several Axes call sites do not handle that StopIteration.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/cbook/__init__.py::_safe_first_finite uses next(...) over a generator (return next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val))). If the iterable contains no finite values (for example [np.nan], or an iterable whose elements are all non-finite), _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration. Callers of this helper must therefore treat the "no finite element" case as a possible outcome.

2) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._convert_dx calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) while only catching (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) around those calls (the except clauses do not include StopIteration). When x (or the supplied inputs) contain no finite element, _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration which propagates out of _convert_dx and can cause ax.bar to abort with StopIteration.

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:broken_barh calls cbook._safe_first_finite(xranges) and cbook._safe_first_finite(yrange) without guarding StopIteration. These call sites share the same probe-for-first-finite-element pattern and thus are susceptible to StopIteration for all-nonfinite inputs.

4) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:_upcast_err (errorbar internals) calls cbook._safe_first_finite(err) inline (used inside isinstance(...) and type(...)) without handling StopIteration; an err sequence with only non-finite values will result in StopIteration propagating from this probe.

5) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:_parse_scatter_color_args (scatter) calls cbook._safe_first_finite(c) inline to detect the element type (e.g., whether the first element is a string) without handling StopIteration; a color sequence leading _safe_first_finite to raise StopIteration will cause scatter to raise.

6) Some other modules already handle StopIteration defensively: lib/matplotlib/dates.py:default_units and lib/matplotlib/units.py:Registry.get_converter both call cbook._safe_first_finite inside try/except that includes StopIteration. This shows mixed handling across the codebase and confirms the pattern (probe first finite element) is used in multiple places.

7) Downstream autoscaling and patch-limit update code (lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py:update_datalim and related _update_patch_limits) explicitly check for finite coordinates (e.g., return if not np.any(np.isfinite(xys))) and thus tolerate NaN coordinates; therefore the observable crash is due to the unguarded probing callers raising StopIteration, not an inability of downstream code to handle NaNs.

8) Investigation gap: the original investigator did not run runtime tests after inspecting the code to confirm end-to-end behavior for all affected call sites; there may be additional callers of _safe_first_finite elsewhere that similarly lack StopIteration handling.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import numpy as np; import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; f, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan])"
Alternatively, in an interactive session:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan])
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Calling ax.bar with x and/or height sequences that contain only NaN should return a BarCollection (with Rectangle(s) whose x and/or height are NaN) and should not raise StopIteration. More generally, API calls should not raise StopIteration for iterables containing only non-finite elements; caller-side probes for a "first finite" element should treat the absence of such an element as a valid outcome rather than allowing StopIteration to escape to the user.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan]) raises StopIteration. The traceback shows StopIteration originating in cbook._safe_first_finite and propagating from lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._convert_dx, causing the call to abort rather than returning a BarCollection.

"""
Core functions and attributes for the matplotlib style library:

``use``
    Select style sheet to override the current matplotlib settings.
``context``
    Context manager to use a style sheet temporarily.
``available``
    List available style sheets.
``library``
    A dictionary of style names and matplotlib settings.
"""

import contextlib
import logging
import os
from pathlib import Path
import re
import warnings

import matplotlib as mpl
from matplotlib import _api, _docstring, rc_params_from_file, rcParamsDefault

_log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

__all__ = ['use', 'context', 'available', 'library', 'reload_library']


@_api.caching_module_getattr  # module-level deprecations
class __getattr__:
    STYLE_FILE_PATTERN = _api.deprecated("3.5", obj_type="")(property(
        lambda self: re.compile(r'([\S]+).%s$' % STYLE_EXTENSION)))


BASE_LIBRARY_PATH = os.path.join(mpl.get_data_path(), 'stylelib')
# Users may want multiple library paths, so store a list of paths.
USER_LIBRARY_PATHS = [os.path.join(mpl.get_configdir(), 'stylelib')]
STYLE_EXTENSION = 'mplstyle'
# A list of rcParams that should not be applied from styles
STYLE_BLACKLIST = {
    'interactive', 'backend', 'webagg.port', 'webagg.address',
    'webagg.port_retries', 'webagg.open_in_browser', 'backend_fallback',
    'toolbar', 'timezone', 'figure.max_open_warning',
    'figure.raise_window', 'savefig.directory', 'tk.window_focus',
    'docstring.hardcopy', 'date.epoch'}


def _remove_blacklisted_style_params(d, warn=True):
    o = {}
    for key in d:  # prevent triggering RcParams.__getitem__('backend')
        if key in STYLE_BLACKLIST:
            if warn:
                _api.warn_external(
                    f"Style includes a parameter, {key!r}, that is not "
                    "related to style.  Ignoring this parameter.")
        else:
            o[key] = d[key]
    return o


def _apply_style(d, warn=True):
    mpl.rcParams.update(_remove_blacklisted_style_params(d, warn=warn))


@_docstring.Substitution(
    "\n".join(map("- {}".format, sorted(STYLE_BLACKLIST, key=str.lower)))
)
def use(style):
    """
    Use Matplotlib style settings from a style specification.

    The style name of 'default' is reserved for reverting back to
    the default style settings.

    .. note::

       This updates the `.rcParams` with the settings from the style.
       `.rcParams` not defined in the style are kept.

    Parameters
    ----------
    style : str, dict, Path or list
        A style specification. Valid options are:

        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | str  | The name of a style or a path/URL to a style file. For a    |
        |      | list of available style names, see `.style.available`.      |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | dict | Dictionary with valid key/value pairs for                   |
        |      | `matplotlib.rcParams`.                                      |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | Path | A path-like object which is a path to a style file.         |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | list | A list of style specifiers (str, Path or dict) applied from |
        |      | first to last in the list.                                  |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+

    Notes
    -----
    The following `.rcParams` are not related to style and will be ignored if
    found in a style specification:

    %s
    """
    if isinstance(style, (str, Path)) or hasattr(style, 'keys'):
        # If name is a single str, Path or dict, make it a single element list.
        styles = [style]
    else:
        styles = style

    style_alias = {'mpl20': 'default', 'mpl15': 'classic'}

    def fix_style(s):
        if isinstance(s, str):
            s = style_alias.get(s, s)
            if s in [
                "seaborn",
                "seaborn-bright",
                "seaborn-colorblind",
                "seaborn-dark",
                "seaborn-darkgrid",
                "seaborn-dark-palette",
                "seaborn-deep",
                "seaborn-muted",
                "seaborn-notebook",
                "seaborn-paper",
                "seaborn-pastel",
                "seaborn-poster",
                "seaborn-talk",
                "seaborn-ticks",
                "seaborn-white",
                "seaborn-whitegrid",
            ]:
                _api.warn_deprecated(
                    "3.6", message="The seaborn styles shipped by Matplotlib "
                    "are deprecated since %(since)s, as they no longer "
                    "correspond to the styles shipped by seaborn. However, "
                    "they will remain available as 'seaborn-v0_8-<style>'. "
                    "Alternatively, directly use the seaborn API instead.")
                s = s.replace("seaborn", "seaborn-v0_8")
        return s

    for style in map(fix_style, styles):
        if not isinstance(style, (str, Path)):
            _apply_style(style)
        elif style == 'default':
            # Deprecation warnings were already handled when creating
            # rcParamsDefault, no need to reemit them here.
            with _api.suppress_matplotlib_deprecation_warning():
                _apply_style(rcParamsDefault, warn=False)
        elif style in library:
            _apply_style(library[style])
        else:
            try:
                rc = rc_params_from_file(style, use_default_template=False)
                _apply_style(rc)
            except IOError as err:
                raise IOError(
                    "{!r} not found in the style library and input is not a "
                    "valid URL or path; see `style.available` for list of "
                    "available styles".format(style)) from err


@contextlib.contextmanager
def context(style, after_reset=False):
    """
    Context manager for using style settings temporarily.

    Parameters
    ----------
    style : str, dict, Path or list
        A style specification. Valid options are:

        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | str  | The name of a style or a path/URL to a style file. For a    |
        |      | list of available style names, see `.style.available`.      |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | dict | Dictionary with valid key/value pairs for                   |
        |      | `matplotlib.rcParams`.                                      |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | Path | A path-like object which is a path to a style file.         |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
        | list | A list of style specifiers (str, Path or dict) applied from |
        |      | first to last in the list.                                  |
        +------+-------------------------------------------------------------+

    after_reset : bool
        If True, apply style after resetting settings to their defaults;
        otherwise, apply style on top of the current settings.
    """
    with mpl.rc_context():
        if after_reset:
            mpl.rcdefaults()
        use(style)
        yield


@_api.deprecated("3.5")
def load_base_library():
    """Load style library defined in this package."""
    library = read_style_directory(BASE_LIBRARY_PATH)
    return library


@_api.deprecated("3.5")
def iter_user_libraries():
    for stylelib_path in USER_LIBRARY_PATHS:
        stylelib_path = os.path.expanduser(stylelib_path)
        if os.path.exists(stylelib_path) and os.path.isdir(stylelib_path):
            yield stylelib_path


def update_user_library(library):
    """Update style library with user-defined rc files."""
    for stylelib_path in map(os.path.expanduser, USER_LIBRARY_PATHS):
        styles = read_style_directory(stylelib_path)
        update_nested_dict(library, styles)
    return library


def read_style_directory(style_dir):
    """Return dictionary of styles defined in *style_dir*."""
    styles = dict()
    for path in Path(style_dir).glob(f"*.{STYLE_EXTENSION}"):
        with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as warns:
            styles[path.stem] = rc_params_from_file(
                path, use_default_template=False)
        for w in warns:
            _log.warning('In %s: %s', path, w.message)
    return styles


def update_nested_dict(main_dict, new_dict):
    """
    Update nested dict (only level of nesting) with new values.

    Unlike `dict.update`, this assumes that the values of the parent dict are
    dicts (or dict-like), so you shouldn't replace the nested dict if it
    already exists. Instead you should update the sub-dict.
    """
    # update named styles specified by user
    for name, rc_dict in new_dict.items():
        main_dict.setdefault(name, {}).update(rc_dict)
    return main_dict


# Load style library
# ==================
_base_library = read_style_directory(BASE_LIBRARY_PATH)
library = None
available = []


def reload_library():
    """Reload the style library."""
    global library
    library = update_user_library(_base_library)
    available[:] = sorted(library.keys())


reload_library()

"""
`matplotlib.pyplot` is a state-based interface to matplotlib. It provides
an implicit,  MATLAB-like, way of plotting.  It also opens figures on your
screen, and acts as the figure GUI manager.

pyplot is mainly intended for interactive plots and simple cases of
programmatic plot generation::

    import numpy as np
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

    x = np.arange(0, 5, 0.1)
    y = np.sin(x)
    plt.plot(x, y)

pyplot is still usually used to create the figure and often the axes in the
figure. See `.pyplot.figure`, `.pyplot.subplots`, and
`.pyplot.subplot_mosaic` to create figures, and
:doc:`Axes API </api/axes_api>` for the plotting methods on an Axes::

def _copy_docstring_and_deprecators(method, func=None):
    if func is None:
        return functools.partial(_copy_docstring_and_deprecators, method)
    decorators = [_docstring.copy(method)]
    # Check whether the definition of *method* includes @_api.rename_parameter
    # or @_api.make_keyword_only decorators; if so, propagate them to the
    # pyplot wrapper as well.
    while getattr(method, "__wrapped__", None) is not None:
        decorator = _api.deprecation.DECORATORS.get(method)
        if decorator:
            decorators.append(decorator)
        method = method.__wrapped__
    for decorator in decorators[::-1]:
        func = decorator(func)
    return func

# The state controlled by {,un}install_repl_displayhook().
_ReplDisplayHook = Enum("_ReplDisplayHook", ["NONE", "PLAIN", "IPYTHON"])
_REPL_DISPLAYHOOK = _ReplDisplayHook.NONE


def _draw_all_if_interactive():
    if matplotlib.is_interactive():
        draw_all()


def install_repl_displayhook():
    """
    Connect to the display hook of the current shell.

    The display hook gets called when the read-evaluate-print-loop (REPL) of
    the shell has finished the execution of a command. We use this callback
    to be able to automatically update a figure in interactive mode.

    This works both with IPython and with vanilla python shells.
    """
    global _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK

    if _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK is _ReplDisplayHook.IPYTHON:
        return

    # See if we have IPython hooks around, if so use them.
    # Use ``sys.modules.get(name)`` rather than ``name in sys.modules`` as
    # entries can also have been explicitly set to None.
    mod_ipython = sys.modules.get("IPython")
    if not mod_ipython:
        _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK = _ReplDisplayHook.PLAIN
        return
    ip = mod_ipython.get_ipython()
    if not ip:
        _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK = _ReplDisplayHook.PLAIN
        return

    ip.events.register("post_execute", _draw_all_if_interactive)
    _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK = _ReplDisplayHook.IPYTHON

    from IPython.core.pylabtools import backend2gui
    # trigger IPython's eventloop integration, if available
    ipython_gui_name = backend2gui.get(get_backend())
    if ipython_gui_name:
        ip.enable_gui(ipython_gui_name)


def uninstall_repl_displayhook():
    """Disconnect from the display hook of the current shell."""
    global _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK
    if _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK is _ReplDisplayHook.IPYTHON:
        from IPython import get_ipython
        ip = get_ipython()
        ip.events.unregister("post_execute", _draw_all_if_interactive)
    _REPL_DISPLAYHOOK = _ReplDisplayHook.NONE

def switch_backend(newbackend):
    """
    Set the pyplot backend.

    Switching to an interactive backend is possible only if no event loop for
    another interactive backend has started.  Switching to and from
    non-interactive backends is always possible.

    If the new backend is different than the current backend then all open
    Figures will be closed via ``plt.close('all')``.

    ----------

    import matplotlib.backends

        current_framework = cbook._get_running_interactive_framework()
        mapping = {'qt': 'qtagg',
                   'gtk3': 'gtk3agg',
                   'gtk4': 'gtk4agg',
                   'wx': 'wxagg',
                   'tk': 'tkagg',
                   'macosx': 'macosx',
                   'headless': 'agg'}

        best_guess = mapping.get(current_framework, None)
        if best_guess is not None:
            candidates = [best_guess]
        else:
            candidates = []
        candidates += [
            "macosx", "qtagg", "gtk4agg", "gtk3agg", "tkagg", "wxagg"]

        # Don't try to fallback on the cairo-based backends as they each have
        # an additional dependency (pycairo) over the agg-based backend, and
        # are of worse quality.
        for candidate in candidates:
            try:
                switch_backend(candidate)
            except ImportError:
                continue
            else:
                rcParamsOrig['backend'] = candidate
                return
        else:
            # Switching to Agg should always succeed; if it doesn't, let the
            # exception propagate out.
            switch_backend("agg")
            rcParamsOrig["backend"] = "agg"
            return
    # have to escape the switch on access logic
    old_backend = dict.__getitem__(rcParams, 'backend')

    backend_mod = importlib.import_module(
        cbook._backend_module_name(newbackend))

    required_framework = _get_required_interactive_framework(backend_mod)
    if required_framework is not None:
        current_framework = cbook._get_running_interactive_framework()
        if (current_framework and required_framework
                and current_framework != required_framework):
            raise ImportError(
                "Cannot load backend {!r} which requires the {!r} interactive "
                "framework, as {!r} is currently running".format(
                    newbackend, required_framework, current_framework))

    # Load the new_figure_manager() and show() functions from the backend.

    # Classically, backends can directly export these functions.  This should
    # keep working for backcompat.
    new_figure_manager = getattr(backend_mod, "new_figure_manager", None)
    # show = getattr(backend_mod, "show", None)
    # In that classical approach, backends are implemented as modules, but
    # "inherit" default method implementations from backend_bases._Backend.
    # This is achieved by creating a "class" that inherits from
    # backend_bases._Backend and whose body is filled with the module globals.
    class backend_mod(matplotlib.backend_bases._Backend):
        locals().update(vars(backend_mod))

    # However, the newer approach for defining new_figure_manager (and, in
    # the future, show) is to derive them from canvas methods.  In that case,
    # also update backend_mod accordingly; also, per-backend customization of
    # draw_if_interactive is disabled.
    if new_figure_manager is None:
        # only try to get the canvas class if have opted into the new scheme
        canvas_class = backend_mod.FigureCanvas
        def new_figure_manager_given_figure(num, figure):
            return canvas_class.new_manager(figure, num)

        def new_figure_manager(num, *args, FigureClass=Figure, **kwargs):
            fig = FigureClass(*args, **kwargs)
            return new_figure_manager_given_figure(num, fig)

        def draw_if_interactive():
            if matplotlib.is_interactive():
                manager = _pylab_helpers.Gcf.get_active()
                if manager:
                    manager.canvas.draw_idle()

        backend_mod.new_figure_manager_given_figure = \
            new_figure_manager_given_figure
        backend_mod.new_figure_manager = new_figure_manager
        backend_mod.draw_if_interactive = draw_if_interactive

    _log.debug("Loaded backend %s version %s.",
               newbackend, backend_mod.backend_version)

    rcParams['backend'] = rcParamsDefault['backend'] = newbackend
    _backend_mod = backend_mod
    for func_name in ["new_figure_manager", "draw_if_interactive", "show"]:
        globals()[func_name].__signature__ = inspect.signature(
            getattr(backend_mod, func_name))

    # Need to keep a global reference to the backend for compatibility reasons.
    # See https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/6092
    matplotlib.backends.backend = newbackend
    if not cbook._str_equal(old_backend, newbackend):
        close("all")

    # make sure the repl display hook is installed in case we become
    # interactive
    install_repl_displayhook()


def _warn_if_gui_out_of_main_thread():
    warn = False
    if _get_required_interactive_framework(_get_backend_mod()):
        if hasattr(threading, 'get_native_id'):
            # This compares native thread ids because even if Python-level
            # Thread objects match, the underlying OS thread (which is what
            # really matters) may be different on Python implementations with
            # green threads.
            if threading.get_native_id() != threading.main_thread().native_id:
                warn = True
        else:
            # Fall back to Python-level Thread if native IDs are unavailable,
            # mainly for PyPy.
            if threading.current_thread() is not threading.main_thread():
                warn = True
    if warn:
        _api.warn_external(
            "Starting a Matplotlib GUI outside of the main thread will likely "
            "fail.")

    ----------
    block : bool, optional
        Whether to wait for all figures to be closed before returning.

        If `True` block and run the GUI main loop until all figure windows
        are closed.

        immediately.  In this case, you are responsible for ensuring
        that the event loop is running to have responsive figures.

        Defaults to True in non-interactive mode and to False in interactive

class classproperty:
    """
    Like `property`, but also triggers on access via the class, and it is the
    *class* that's passed as argument.

    Examples
    --------
    ::

        class C:
            @classproperty
            def foo(cls):
                return cls.__name__

            raise ValueError('classproperty only implements fget.')

        self.fdel = fdel

    def __get__(self, instance, owner):
        return self._fget(owner)

    @property
    def fget(self):
        return self._fget

def check_isinstance(_types, **kwargs):
    """

             (none_type,) if types is None else

                else f"{tp.__module__}.{tp.__qualname__}")

                names.remove("None")

                "{!r} must be an instance of {}, not a {}".format(
                    k,
                    ", ".join(names[:-1]) + " or " + names[-1]

    Parameters
    ----------
    _values : iterable
        Sequence of values to check on.
    _print_supported_values : bool, default: True
        Whether to print *_values* when raising ValueError.
    **kwargs : dict
        *key, value* pairs as keyword arguments to find in *_values*.

    Raises
    ------
    ValueError
        If any *value* in *kwargs* is not found in *_values*.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> _api.check_in_list(["foo", "bar"], arg=arg, other_arg=other_arg)
    """
    if not kwargs:
        raise TypeError("No argument to check!")
    values = _values
    for key, val in kwargs.items():
        if val not in values:

    *_shape*, if not, raise an appropriate ValueError.

    e.g. (None, 2) -> (N, 2)

    The values checked must be numpy arrays.

    --------

    for k, v in kwargs.items():
        data_shape = v.shape

        if len(target_shape) != len(data_shape) or any(
                t not in [s, None]
                for t, s in zip(target_shape, data_shape)

            dim_labels = iter(itertools.chain(
                'MNLIJKLH',
                (f"D{i}" for i in itertools.count())))
            text_shape = ", ".join((str(n)
                                    if n is not None
                                    else next(dim_labels)
                                    for n in target_shape))

            raise ValueError(
                f"{k!r} must be {len(target_shape)}D "
                f"with shape ({text_shape}). "
                f"Your input has shape {v.shape}."

def caching_module_getattr(cls):
    """
    Helper decorator for implementing module-level ``__getattr__`` as a class.

    This decorator must be used at the module toplevel as follows::

        @caching_module_getattr
        class __getattr__:  # The class *must* be named ``__getattr__``.
            @property  # Only properties are taken into account.
            def name(self): ...

    The ``__getattr__`` class will be replaced by a ``__getattr__``
    function such that trying to access ``name`` on the module will
    resolve the corresponding property (which may be decorated e.g. with
    ``_api.deprecated`` for deprecating module globals).  The properties are
    all implicitly cached.  Moreover, a suitable AttributeError is generated
    and raised if no property with the given name exists.
    """

    assert cls.__name__ == "__getattr__"
    # Don't accidentally export cls dunders.
    props = {name: prop for name, prop in vars(cls).items()
             if isinstance(prop, property)}
    instance = cls()

    @functools.lru_cache(None)
    def __getattr__(name):
        if name in props:
            return props[name].__get__(instance)
        raise AttributeError(
            f"module {cls.__module__!r} has no attribute {name!r}")

    return __getattr__

    Use as ::

    class so far, an alias named ``get_alias`` will be defined; the same will
    be done for setters.  If neither the getter nor the setter exists, an

    can be used by `.normalize_kwargs` (which assumes that higher priority
    aliases come last).

        return functools.partial(define_aliases, alias_d)

    def make_alias(name):  # Enforce a closure over *name*.
        @functools.wraps(getattr(cls, name))
        def method(self, *args, **kwargs):

    for prop, aliases in alias_d.items():
        exists = False
        for prefix in ["get_", "set_"]:
            if prefix + prop in vars(cls):
                exists = True
                for alias in aliases:
                    method = make_alias(prefix + prop)
                    method.__name__ = prefix + alias
                    method.__doc__ = "Alias for `{}`.".format(prefix + prop)
                    setattr(cls, prefix + alias, method)
        if not exists:
            raise ValueError(
                "Neither getter nor setter exists for {!r}".format(prop))

    def get_aliased_and_aliases(d):
        return {*d, *(alias for aliases in d.values() for alias in aliases)}

    preexisting_aliases = getattr(cls, "_alias_map", {})
    conflicting = (get_aliased_and_aliases(preexisting_aliases)
                   & get_aliased_and_aliases(alias_d))
    if conflicting:
        # Need to decide on conflict resolution policy.
        raise NotImplementedError(
            f"Parent class already defines conflicting aliases: {conflicting}")

Current (lines ~254–261):

Suggested replacement:
```py
def reload_library():
    """Reload the style library."""
    global library
    library = update_user_library(_base_library)

    # Provide backwards-compatible aliases for deprecated seaborn styles:
    # Matplotlib 3.6 renamed the shipped seaborn styles from
    # "seaborn-<style>" to "seaborn-v0_8-<style>". Historically code could
    # index plt.style.library['seaborn-<style>'], so create aliases mapping
    # the old names to the new ones if the old name doesn't already exist
    # (user may have defined custom style with the old name).
    aliases = {}
    for name in list(library.keys()):
        if name.startswith("seaborn-v0_8-"):
            legacy = name.replace("seaborn-v0_8-", "seaborn-")
            if legacy not in library:
                aliases[legacy] = library[name]
    if aliases:
        library.update(aliases)

    available[:] = sorted(library.keys())
```

This adds alias entries to the library dict itself.

Historically available listed the shipped style names; with deprecation it's reasonable to include only canonical names. That is a policy choice. Checkpoint 5: Final report (root cause + complete fix + locations + rationale)

Root cause
- Matplotlib 3.6 changed the canonical names of the seaborn styles it ships from "seaborn-<style>" to "seaborn-v0_8-<style>" and left the mapping from old names -> new names only in use() (which warns and replaces string). As a consequence, plt.style.library no longer contains keys like "seaborn-colorblind", so direct dictionary access plt.style.library["seaborn-colorblind"] raises KeyError. Historically the codebase had these keys available, so user code that relies on direct indexing breaks.

All locations that need to change
- Primary change: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py: reload_library() — add deprecated-to-canonical alias keys into the library dictionary after it is constructed.
  - Current code: sets library = update_user_library(_base_library) and available = sorted(library.keys()).
  - Fix: after constructing library, create aliases mapping "seaborn-<style>" -> library["seaborn-v0_8-<style>"] for each canonical "seaborn-v0_8-<style>" key, only if the legacy name doesn't already exist in library (user-defined). Then set available to sorted(library.keys()) as before.
- No changes required in use() — it already does the deprecation mapping and emits warnings on use() calls.
- No other modules need to be modified; style/__init__.py re-exports library unchanged.
- Optional (not required for correctness): to notify users who access library through direct indexing that the name is deprecated, one could make library a Mapping object that emits deprecation warnings on access to legacy names, but that is more intrusive.

What the current code does, what assumption it encodes, and what the fix should be
- Current code:
  - Assumes that callers will use style.use() to refer to deprecated seaborn names; use() warns and maps to canonical names.
  - The style.library dict contains only the canonical (new) names from the installed stylelib files (e.g., "seaborn-v0_8-colorblind"), not the legacy names.
- Encoded assumption:
  - The assumption is that no code accesses style.library directly using legacy names; or that callers will pass names to use(), which does the mapping for them.
- Fix:
  - Make library also contain legacy keys as aliases (pointing to the same rc dict values) to preserve backward compatibility for direct indexing. Create aliases only if they don't overwrite user-provided names.

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic (if any)
- We used the literal prefix "seaborn-v0_8-" to detect canonical names and create legacy aliases. This corresponds directly to the replacement performed by fix_style() in use(), so it is consistent. If in a future change the canonical prefix changes again, both fix_style() and the alias code must be updated together. There are no numeric hardcoded values or offsets to convert.

Testing / Example
- After patch, the reproduction code:
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  the_rc = plt.style.library["seaborn-colorblind"]
should work again (returning the style rc dictionary) in Matplotlib 3.6.x.

Patch summary (ready-to-apply snippet)
- Replace reload_library() in lib/matplotlib/style/core.py with:

```py
def reload_library():
    """Reload the style library."""
    global library
    library = update_user_library(_base_library)

    # Backwards-compatible aliases for seaborn styles renamed to
    # "seaborn-v0_8-<style>" in Matplotlib >= 3.6: create the legacy keys
    # "seaborn-<style>" pointing to the same rc-dict if the legacy key does
    # not already exist (user may have overridden it).
    aliases = {}
    for name in list(library.keys()):
        if name.startswith("seaborn-v0_8-"):
            legacy = name.replace("seaborn-v0_8-", "seaborn-")
            if legacy not in library:
                aliases[legacy] = library[name]
    if aliases:
        library.update(aliases)

    available[:] = sorted(library.keys())
```

Notes and rationale
- This change is minimal, local to reload_library(), and restores backward compatibility for code that directly indexes style.library with the legacy seaborn names.
- It preserves the deprecation warnings in style.use() (so code that uses style.use('seaborn-colorblind') will still get the deprecation message and be nudged to change). The aliasing only reintroduces the keys for direct dict access; it does not suppress deprecation warnings that are intentionally emitted by use().
- It avoids overwriting user-provided legacy names by checking "if legacy not in library".
- Optionally, if the maintainers prefer to avoid adding deprecated names to style.available, we could add the aliases only to library but not to available. The change above adds them to both library and available (consistent with prior behavior and what callers likely expect).
- If maintainers want to also emit deprecation warnings on direct access to legacy keys (plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind']), then a more involved change is required: replace `library` with a custom Mapping object that wraps the internal dict and emits warnings on __getitem__ for legacy names. That's more invasive and wasn't requested by the bug report; the user expects direct access to function as before, so the alias approach is the most straightforward compatibility fix.

===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: plt.style.library no longer contains legacy seaborn keys (e.g. "seaborn-colorblind"); direct lookup plt.style.library["seaborn-colorblind"] raises KeyError on Matplotlib 3.6.x even though style.use("seaborn-colorblind") is supported (with a deprecation warning). The wrong assumption is that deprecated names only need mapping in use(), not also as keys in library.
- Root cause (file/method): lib/matplotlib/style/core.py — reload_library() populates the module-level dict library from installed *.mplstyle files (canonical names are "seaborn-v0_8-..."), while use()’s fix_style() remaps legacy names like "seaborn-colorblind" to "seaborn-v0_8-colorblind" (and warns) only at call time. (Finding produced in Checkpoint 1c/2a.)
- Fix strategy: In reload_library(), after building library = update_user_library(_base_library), create backward-compatible aliases mapping each "seaborn-v0_8-<style>" -> "seaborn-<style>" (only if legacy key doesn't already exist). Update available accordingly. This restores direct-dict access and preserves existing deprecation behavior in use(). (Proposed in Checkpoint 4.)
- Hardcoded constants to watch: the prefix "seaborn-v0_8-" (used by fix_style() replacement) — aliasing logic must match exactly the same replacement rule. The explicit legacy-name list inside fix_style() also encodes the deprecated names.
- Methods to add/override: none required; change is local to reload_library(). Optionally, making library a custom Mapping that warns on deprecated-key access is a more invasive alternative (not required for correctness). (Decision in Checkpoint 4e.)
- Gaps in investigation: agent did not implement or test an access-time deprecation warning (i.e., making library a wrapper that warns on plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind']). Also didn’t inspect user-config path merging edge-cases where user style overrides could conflict with aliasing — must ensure aliases do not overwrite user-defined legacy keys (fixed in proposed code). (Gap noted in Checkpoint 4 and 3a.)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: library populated with canonical names only (KeyError root cause)
What: reload_library() in lib/matplotlib/style/core.py builds library from installed style files; installed files use canonical names "seaborn-v0_8-<style>", so library lacks "seaborn-<style>" keys and direct lookup KeyError occurs. (core.py:reload_library). (Discovered Checkpoint 1c / 2a)
Why it matters: user code doing plt.style.library["seaborn-colorblind"] breaks; use() remapping does not help direct dict access.

### Finding 2: use() maps legacy -> canonical and warns (but does not affect library keys)
What: use() contains an inner fix_style(s) that, for an explicit list of seaborn names (including "seaborn-colorblind"), calls _api.warn_deprecated(...) and does s = s.replace("seaborn", "seaborn-v0_8"). (core.py:use / fix_style). (Discovered Checkpoint 1c / 3a)
Why it matters: That mapping explains why style.use('seaborn-colorblind') still works, and it shows the exact replacement rule ("seaborn" -> "seaborn-v0_8") to be mirrored for library aliases.

### Finding 3: reload_library() is the single minimal place to add aliases
What: reload_library() constructs library = update_user_library(_base_library) and then sets available[:] = sorted(library.keys()). Modifying reload_library() to add aliases after library is built is minimal and safe. (core.py:reload_library). (Discovered Checkpoint 1c / 4)
Why it matters: A local change here fixes direct dict access for all callers and is least invasive.

### Finding 4: Must not overwrite user-defined names (merge rule)
What: users can place custom .mplstyle files under USER_LIBRARY_PATHS; reload_library() merges them into library. Aliasing code must only add legacy keys when legacy not already present (if a user defined a legacy key, do not overwrite it). (core.py:reload_library and USER_LIBRARY_PATHS). (Discovered Checkpoint 3a / 4)
Why it matters: Prevents surprising overwrite of user styles.

### Finding 5: Hardcoded values to keep in sync
What: The canonical prefix "seaborn-v0_8-" and the explicit legacy list inside fix_style() are the hardcoded rules that determine mapping. Alias generation must be consistent with fix_style()’s replacement. (core.py:use / fix_style). (Discovered Checkpoint 1c / 3a)
Why it matters: If either is changed in the future, both places must be updated; otherwise aliases or use() mapping will diverge.

### Finding 6: Gap — direct-access deprecation signaling not implemented
What: The investigation did not implement or test emitting a deprecation warning on direct library access (plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind']). Adding aliases silently restores access but does not warn on direct dict access. (Gap noted in Checkpoint 4e)
Why it matters: Maintainers may want to continue nudging users away from legacy keys; achieving that requires a wrapper Mapping with __getitem__ warnings — more invasive than aliasing.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:reload_library
- Wrong assumption: reload_library assumes the set of keys in library need only include canonical (installed) names (e.g., "seaborn-v0_8-colorblind"); it assumes callers will use style.use() to perform legacy-to-canonical mapping.
- Evidence:
  - Current code sets library = update_user_library(_base_library) then available[:] = sorted(library.keys()). (core.py lines near 247–261)
  - Reproduction: plt.style.library["seaborn-colorblind"] -> KeyError on Matplotlib 3.6.1 because library lacks "seaborn-colorblind" while installed library contains "seaborn-v0_8-colorblind".
  - The module also defines BASE_LIBRARY_PATH and USER_LIBRARY_PATHS, showing library is driven by installed/stylelib files.
- Related locations:
  - lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:use (fix_style) — uses replacement "seaborn" -> "seaborn-v0_8"
  - USER_LIBRARY_PATHS handling code that merges user styles via update_user_library

### Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:use / fix_style
- Wrong assumption: fix_style encodes that mapping legacy->canonical belongs in use() only (i.e., name mapping will be applied at use-time, not by library itself).
- Evidence:
  - fix_style contains an explicit list of legacy names (including "seaborn-colorblind") and does s = s.replace("seaborn", "seaborn-v0_8") plus _api.warn_deprecated(...) before lookup. Exact code: replace("seaborn", "seaborn-v0_8").
  - Because fix_style maps to canonical names, use() succeeds even though library lacks legacy keys.
- Related locations:
  - lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:reload_library (where library keys are defined)
  - Any external code that reads plt.style.library directly (user code that failed)

### Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:BASE_LIBRARY_PATH / STYLE_EXTENSION
- Wrong assumption: style files and their canonical names are the single source of truth for available keys; code relies on installed stylelib contents.
- Evidence:
  - BASE_LIBRARY_PATH = os.path.join(mpl.get_data_path(), 'stylelib') and STYLE_EXTENSION = 'mplstyle' drive read_style_directory; read_style_directory returns mapping path.stem -> rc_params_from_file(path,...), so keys are derived from filenames inside installed stylelib (these filenames are the canonical "seaborn-v0_8-..." filenames).
- Related locations:
  - read_style_directory, update_user_library, reload_library
