Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (concrete code changes, complete):

1) lib/matplotlib/legend.py
   - Add a __getstate__ method to Legend that ensures the Draggable helper is disconnected and removed from the pickled state.

Patch sketch:

In class Legend (add method):

    def __getstate__(self):
        d = super().__getstate__()
        # If there is a draggable helper attached, disconnect it so we don't
        # try to pickle the GUI canvas stored on the helper. Store None in
        # the state to avoid pickling the helper.
        if getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
            try:
                # disconnect callbacks on the live object to avoid leaving
                # dangling callback registrations on the canvas
                self._draggable.disconnect()
            except Exception:
                # Be defensive: ignore errors during state capture
                pass
            d["_draggable"] = None
        return d

Notes:
- We call disconnect() on the live object (self._draggable) before returning state. This cleans up connections to the canvas on the live figure (prevents dangling callbacks).
- We replace _draggable in the returned state with None to prevent pickle from trying to serialize the Draggable helper (which references the unpicklable canvas).

2) lib/matplotlib/text.py
   - Extend Text.__getstate__ to also clear and disconnect any _draggable helper (annotations use _draggable via _AnnotationBase).

Patch sketch (modify Text.__getstate__):

    def __getstate__(self):
        d = super().__getstate__()
        # remove the cached _renderer (if it exists)
        d['_renderer'] = None
        # if this Text/Annotation has a draggable helper, disconnect it and
        # don't include it in the pickled state (it holds a canvas).
        if getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
            try:
                self._draggable.disconnect()
            except Exception:
                pass
            d["_draggable"] = None
        return d

Rationale: Both Legend and Annotation use Draggable helpers which store a reference to the figure canvas; by disconnecting and removing these helpers during pickling we avoid serializing the canvas and avoid pickling errors.

Now answer the checkpoint 4 follow-up questions in detail, referencing concrete code:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence answer: The current code implicitly assumes that any helper object attached to an Artist (e.g., Legend._draggable or Text._draggable) can be safely pickled along with the artist; the fix removes this assumption by treating Draggable helpers as non-picklable runtime-only state (disconnect them and exclude from pickled state).

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- Files/components with hardcoded values I inspected (from Checkpoint 3a) include:
  - DraggableBase: uses canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas and registers callbacks for signals "pick_event", "button_release_event", "motion_notify_event". These are literal strings but are not the cause of the pickling failure by themselves.
- Assessment with respect to the assumption:
  - The problem is not a numeric constant but the presence of storing the canvas object (self.canvas = figure.canvas) and registering callbacks to it. That is a runtime, non-picklable object stored as instance state. That storage is what encoded the assumption that the helper can be pickled. There are no numeric constants here to make dynamic — the fix is to remove these references from pickled state.
  - None of the numeric hardcoded constants in offsetbox.py (padding defaults etc.) depend on the assumption we are changing. They remain correct and do not need to become dynamic.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- Reverse operation in this context: unpickling. We must ensure unpickling either reinitializes necessary runtime state or that it's safe to not have it. Specifically:
  - The Figure.__setstate__ re-creates a FigureCanvasBase(self) (see figure.py: FigureCanvasBase(self) in __setstate__) to set self.canvas.
  - Because we will remove _draggable from the pickled Legend/Text state, unpickled legends/annotations will not have their helper re-created automatically. That means a legend that had been draggable in the original live figure will not be draggable after unpickling automatically.
  - Check if any inherited parent method expects _draggable to be present on load: Artist __setstate__ is generic; there are no automatic re-attachment rules to recreate _draggable. The current behavior prior to this change probably also would have failed to pickle at all (so no expectation of restoring draggability). So dropping _draggable is acceptable. If desired as a future feature, one could store a boolean flag and recreate the Draggable helper on demand when figure is reattached to a canvas — but that's outside the scope here.

To be explicit: trace the complementary methods that are involved in pickling / unpickling:

- Parent methods involved: Artist.__getstate__ returns a dict of __dict__ with stale_callback set to None. Figure.__getstate__ then pops "canvas". If Legend/Text keep _draggable references, the pickler will attempt to pickle them and fail because they reference canvas. By ensuring Legend.__getstate__/Text.__getstate__ set _draggable to None in the returned state, unpickling will not attempt to reconstruct Draggable helpers. After unpickling, Figure.__setstate__ creates a new canvas via FigureCanvasBase(self), so the unpickled Figure has a canvas again. But since _draggable is None, the draggable functionality is not restored automatically — which is consistent with the fact that the helper stores live GUI callbacks that cannot be restored purely from pickled data.

- Should the primary class override other parent methods? No parent method performs the complementary operation that relies on a persistent Draggable helper, so no additional overrides are required. However, disconnecting in __getstate__ ensures live helper's callbacks are removed, which avoids leaving the old canvas with dangling callbacks (good).

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Legend methods:
  - set_draggable / get_draggable will still work at runtime. If you set legend.set_draggable(True) after unpickling, it will create a new DraggableLegend and reattach callbacks to the current canvas.
  - draw and other layout methods are unchanged.
  - The only behavioral difference is that a legend that was draggable before pickling will not be automatically re-draggable on unpickle; the user can call legend.set_draggable(True) after unpickling. This is reasonable since the draggable helper is runtime-only (holds canvas).
- Text/Annotation methods:
  - draggable() still works as before; only pickling path is changed.
  - No method uses persistent Draggable helper across pickling, so no functional issues besides "draggability not preserved by pickling" which is acceptable and preferable to raising TypeError on pickle.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- I evaluated parent class methods that might need to be overridden to complement the change (e.g., to re-create _draggable on unpickle automatically). The parent class (Artist) defines __getstate__/__setstate__ but not drag-specific behavior. We modified Legend.__getstate__ and Text.__getstate__.
- Optionally, if someone wants dragging to be preserved across pickling, we'd need:
  - To store a picklable representation (e.g., a boolean flag like _draggable_was_set and the 'update' parameter) in the pickled state.
  - And upon unpickling (in __setstate__), detect that flag and if figure.canvas is available, re-create the helper (call set_draggable(True) with saved options).
  - This would require a Legend.__setstate__ override. I chose NOT to implement automatic re-creation because:
    - The Draggable helper references live GUI canvas and callbacks which are environment-specific; automatically reattaching callbacks during __setstate__ may be surprising and not always desired.
    - The simplest robust fix for the reported bug is to prevent unpicklable canvas object from being included in the state; preserve draggability is a separate enhancement.
- So no new method is required for correctness. If preserving draggability across pickling is desired, we would add:
  - In __getstate__: store a boolean (e.g., d['_draggable_was_set'] = True and d['_draggable_opts'] = {'use_blit': ..., 'update': ...})
  - In __setstate__: if _draggable_was_set and self.figure.canvas is available, call self.set_draggable(True, **_draggable_opts). But this requires caution: __setstate__ runs before Figure.__setstate__ sets up canvas? In Figure.__setstate__ the FigureCanvasBase(self) call recreates canvas after the Figure __dict__ is assigned (see figure.__setstate__). For Legend's __setstate__ we need to ensure figure.canvas exists; implement in Legend.__setstate__ or in Figure.__setstate__ after canvas creation reattach — important but beyond this bugfix. Given scope, we will not implement automatic re-creation.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis and concrete fixes)

Root cause
- Calling Legend.set_draggable(True) (or Annotation.draggable(True)) attaches a Draggable* helper object (DraggableLegend or DraggableAnnotation) to the Artist under attribute _draggable. DraggableBase stores a reference to the GUI FigureCanvas (self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas) and callback connections to it. The FigureCanvas (e.g., FigureCanvasQTAgg) is not picklable. Legend/Text did not remove _draggable when being pickled, so pickle attempts to serialize the canvas via the helper and fails with TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object.

Every location that needs to change
1) lib/matplotlib/legend.py (Legend class)
   - Current behavior: Legend has no __getstate__. When pickled, Legend._draggable (if set) is included in the object's state; this leads to pickling the Draggable helper and through it the unpicklable canvas.
   - Fix: add Legend.__getstate__ which:
     - Calls super().__getstate__() to get state dict
     - If self._draggable is not None:
       - call self._draggable.disconnect() to remove runtime callbacks from the live canvas
       - set d['_draggable'] = None (or remove the key) in the returned state so the helper (and its canvas) is not pickled
     - Return the modified state dict
   - Rationale: Draggable helper is runtime-only; disconnect and exclude it from pickled state to avoid pickling canvas.

2) lib/matplotlib/text.py (Text class)
   - Current behavior: Text.__getstate__ clears cached _renderer only; it does not clear or disconnect _draggable for Annotation/Text.
   - Fix: modify Text.__getstate__ to also:
     - If getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
       - call self._draggable.disconnect()
       - set d['_draggable'] = None
     - Keep existing behavior that sets d['_renderer'] = None
   - Rationale: Annotation uses the same DraggableBase helper (DraggableAnnotation) and must also avoid pickling the helper's canvas.

Why these two locations are sufficient:
- Legend and Annotation are the public places where set_draggable/draggable attach a Draggable helper on the instance via attribute _draggable. There are no other classes in the tree that set a persistent _draggable attribute that hold a canvas reference (search for "_draggable" turned up only these locations). The Draggable* helpers themselves should not be pickled because they reference the live canvas — removing them from the pickled state removes the offending reference.

Code examples (exact changes to make)

1) lib/matplotlib/legend.py — add __getstate__:

Insert into class Legend:

    def __getstate__(self):
        d = super().__getstate__()
        # Disconnect and remove the runtime-only Draggable helper that stores
        # a reference to the figure canvas (not picklable).
        if getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
            try:
                self._draggable.disconnect()
            except Exception:
                pass
            d["_draggable"] = None
        return d

2) lib/matplotlib/text.py — modify __getstate__ in Text:

Replace current __getstate__ with:

    def __getstate__(self):
        d = super().__getstate__()
        # remove the cached _renderer (if it exists)
        d['_renderer'] = None
        # If the text/annotation is draggable, disconnect the runtime helper
        # (which stores a reference to the canvas) and don't include it in the
        # pickled state.
        if getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
            try:
                self._draggable.disconnect()
            except Exception:
                pass
            d["_draggable"] = None
        return d

Notes and side effects
- Behavior change: Draggability is not preserved automatically by pickling/unpickling. After unpickling, the legend/annotation will not be draggable even if it had been before pickling. The user can call legend.set_draggable(True) or annotation.draggable(True) after unpickling to re-enable moving.
- This is a conservative, minimal and robust fix: it prevents pickle errors and avoids trying to serialize GUI objects that cannot be serialized reliably across backends or sessions.
- If we wanted to preserve the draggable state across pickling, we would need to:
  - Store picklable metadata in __getstate__ (e.g., a boolean and the parameters used for set_draggable),
  - Re-create the helper in __setstate__ after canvas is restored — but this requires careful ordering with Figure.__setstate__ (Figure.__setstate__ creates the canvas), and also raises potential issues across different backends/environments. It's doable, but beyond the scope of the bug report (which is to fix the TypeError).

Tests to add (recommended)
- Add/extend a unit test that creates a figure with legend.set_draggable(True) and ensures pickle.dumps(fig) succeeds (didn't before).
- Add/extend a test for Annotation.draggable(True) and pickling succeeds.

Summary (short)
- Root cause: Draggable helper objects attached to Legend/Annotation keep a reference to the FigureCanvas (non-picklable). Pickling the figure tries to pickle the canvas via those helpers and fails.
- Fix: Legend.__getstate__ and Text.__getstate__ must remove (and disconnect) the runtime-only _draggable helper from the pickled state (set _draggable to None and call disconnect() to cleanup live callbacks). This prevents pickle from attempting to serialize the FigureCanvas and resolves the TypeError.
- No other hardcoded numeric values required changing. No new methods strictly required; optional enhancement to preserve draggability across pickling would require storing a flag and re-creating the helper in __setstate__ (not implemented here).

If you want, I can provide a concrete patch/diff for the two files showing exact insertions, and also add a short unit test that reproduces the original failure and confirms the fix.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: pickling a Figure with a draggable Legend/Annotation raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object. Wrong assumption: runtime-only GUI objects (FigureCanvas) stored on helper objects attached to artists are picklable.
- Root cause & location: Draggable helper objects (DraggableLegend / DraggableAnnotation) keep a direct reference to the figure canvas (self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas). These helpers are stored on the artist as self._draggable (lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableBase; created by lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend.set_draggable and text/annotation code in lib/matplotlib/text.py).
- Fix strategy: make Legend and Text/Annotation exclude and clean up their runtime-only _draggable helper during pickling by overriding __getstate__ (disconnect callbacks on the live helper and set _draggable = None in the returned state). This prevents pickling the FigureCanvas via helpers.
- Scope: change two locations to be complete and consistent: add Legend.__getstate__ in lib/matplotlib/legend.py and extend Text.__getstate__ in lib/matplotlib/text.py to handle _draggable. No numeric constants need changing.
- Methods to add/override: Legend.__getstate__ (new); Text.__getstate__ (modify existing). Optional enhancement (not implemented here): store a picklable flag and re-create the helper in __setstate__ if preserving draggability across pickle/unpickle is desired.
- Investigation gaps: I did not implement automatic re-creation of draggable helpers on unpickling (would require careful ordering with Figure.__setstate__ and canvas creation). Also no change to DraggableBase internals was made — that’s correct for minimal fix, but if preserving draggability is a requirement, additional work is needed.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Draggable helper holds unpicklable canvas (root cause) — Checkpoint 3
What: DraggableBase stores the GUI canvas on the helper: self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas, and registers callbacks on it. Why it matters: the helper (kept on artist as _draggable) causes pickle to try to serialize FigureCanvasQTAgg and fail. (file: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py, class: DraggableBase, attr/method: __init__ storing self.canvas and connecting callbacks)

### Finding 2: Figure.__getstate__ already removes canvas but that is insufficient — Checkpoint 1 & 3
What: Figure.__getstate__ pops "canvas" from figure state (lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__getstate__), so a bare Figure is picklable; but other objects (artists) can still reference the canvas and cause failure. Why it matters: removing canvas from Figure state alone does not prevent errors if Legend._draggable references the canvas.

### Finding 3: Legend stores Draggable helper on instance and does not exclude it from pickled state — Checkpoint 2
What: Legend.set_draggable creates a DraggableLegend and stores it in Legend._draggable; Legend class has no __getstate__ that removes _draggable. Why it matters: Legend._draggable will be included in pickling and thus propagates the unpicklable canvas. (file: lib/matplotlib/legend.py, class: Legend, method: set_draggable / attribute: _draggable)

### Finding 4: Annotation/Text uses same pattern and also needs fix — Checkpoint 3
What: Annotation code attaches a _draggable helper (via DraggableAnnotation) and Text.__getstate__ currently only clears _renderer; it does not clear/disconnect _draggable. Why it matters: draggable annotations produce the same pickle failure; both Legend and Text/Annotation must be fixed. (file: lib/matplotlib/text.py, class: Text / _AnnotationBase, method: __getstate__ needs extension)

### Finding 5: Minimal robust fix — Checkpoint 4
What: Add Legend.__getstate__ to disconnect and set _draggable = None in state; extend Text.__getstate__ to do the same. Why it matters: removes the canvas reference from pickled state and prevents TypeError. (files: lib/matplotlib/legend.py and lib/matplotlib/text.py)

### Finding 6: Gap — preservation of draggability across pickle/unpickle not implemented — Checkpoint 4
What: Unpickling will not restore draggability automatically because we drop the helper. Why it matters: if users expect draggability to persist across pickling, we must add flags and implement __setstate__ to re-create helpers after Figure.__setstate__ recreates canvas. This was intentionally left out to keep fix minimal and robust.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableBase.__init__
- Wrong assumption: the helper object storing self.canvas is safe to live on the artist instance and therefore safe to pickle with the artist.
- Evidence:
  - Found code: self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas (DraggableBase.__init__).
  - DraggableBase connects callbacks using self.canvas.callbacks._connect_picklable(...), so the helper keeps live GUI references.
  - These canvas objects are concrete backends like FigureCanvasQTAgg (non-picklable).
- Related locations: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableOffsetBox (subclass), lib/matplotlib/legend.py:DraggableLegend (instantiation), lib/matplotlib/text.py:DraggableAnnotation (similar helper for annotations).

(Discovery checkpoint: 3)

---

### Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend (attribute: _draggable; method: set_draggable)
- Wrong assumption: Legend instances can include the runtime _draggable helper in their pickled state.
- Evidence:
  - Legend.set_draggable creates a DraggableLegend and assigns it to self._draggable (seen at legend.py lines around set_draggable).
  - Legend class has no __getstate__ override, so default Artist.__getstate__ will include _draggable in returned state.
  - When _draggable exists, it references a canvas (via DraggableBase), so pickling a Figure containing that Legend raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object.
- Related locations: lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__getstate__ (pops 'canvas' only), lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableBase (creates the canvas reference).

(Discovery checkpoint: 2 and 3)

---

### Location: lib/matplotlib/text.py:Text.__getstate__
- Wrong assumption: Text/Annotation pickling does not need to remove runtime-only helpers (it only clears cached renderer).
- Evidence:
  - Existing code: Text.__getstate__ sets d['_renderer'] = None but does not check or clear d['_draggable'].
  - _AnnotationBase uses self._draggable for draggable annotations; these helpers hold a canvas reference like DraggableBase.
  - Thus, pickling annotations that are draggable hits the same canvas-pickling failure.
- Related locations: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableBase, lib/matplotlib/text.py:_AnnotationBase (where _draggable is created).

(Discovery checkpoint: 3)

---

### Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__getstate__
- Wrong assumption (partial): popping the Figure.canvas from Figure state is sufficient to make Figures picklable in all cases.
- Evidence:
  - Code: Figure.__getstate__ does state.pop("canvas") — this prevents serializing the Figure.canvas attribute itself.
  - However, other objects in the figure (artists) can still hold references to the canvas (e.g., via _draggable helpers), so Figure.__getstate__ alone does not prevent pickle errors.
- Related locations: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend._draggable, lib/matplotlib/text.py:_draggable.

(Discovery checkpoint: 1 & 3)

---

### Recommended minimal code changes (evidence + exact names)
- Add to lib/matplotlib/legend.py (inside class Legend):

    def __getstate__(self):
        d = super().__getstate__()
        if getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
            try:
                self._draggable.disconnect()
            except Exception:
                pass
            d["_draggable"] = None
        return d

- Modify lib/matplotlib/text.py (Text.__getstate__) to append the same _draggable handling:

    def __getstate__(self):
        d = super().__getstate__()
        d['_renderer'] = None
        if getattr(self, "_draggable", None) is not None:
            try:
                self._draggable.disconnect()
            except Exception:
                pass
            d["_draggable"] = None
        return d

(These are the minimal authoritative edits needed to stop the TypeError when pickle.dumps(fig) is called with a draggable legend or annotation. Discovery & decision checkpoint: 4)

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify each claim in the draft report against the source code and the investigation analysis. Below I list what I checked, why, the code evidence, and whether the draft's claims are supported or need adjusting.

1) Verify Draggable helper stores a direct reference to the Figure.canvas and registers callbacks on it
- Why I checked: The draft's root cause depends on the helper holding an unpicklable backend object (FigureCanvas) via a direct reference and callback registration.
- What I inspected: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py around DraggableBase.
- Evidence found:
  - DraggableBase.__init__ stores the canvas:
    - offsetbox.py:1508: self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas
  - DraggableBase registers callback ids using _connect_picklable on that canvas:
    - offsetbox.py:1511-1515:
      - self.canvas.callbacks._connect_picklable('pick_event', self.on_pick)
      - self.canvas.callbacks._connect_picklable('button_release_event', self.on_release)
  - DraggableBase uses self.canvas in drawing/interaction methods (on_motion, on_pick, etc.), e.g. canvas.draw(), canvas.blit(), canvas.copy_from_bbox().
    - offsetbox.py:1523-1528, 1537-1542, 1539 etc.
- Conclusion: The draft claim is correct. The helper holds a direct reference to the backend canvas and connects callbacks on it, which makes the helper reference non-picklable GUI objects (e.g. FigureCanvasQTAgg).

2) Verify Legend stores the helper on the instance and has no special pickling that removes it
- Why I checked: The report claims Legend.set_draggable creates a helper assigned to self._draggable and Legend does not override __getstate__, therefore _draggable will be included in pickled state.
- What I inspected:
  - lib/matplotlib/legend.py: constructor sets self._draggable = None and calls set_draggable:
    - legend.py:614-616: self._draggable = None; self.set_draggable(state=draggable)
  - legend.py:set_draggable assigns the helper:
    - legend.py:1196-1201: if state: if self._draggable is None: self._draggable = DraggableLegend(self, use_blit, update=update)
    - legend.py:1202-1205: else -> disconnect and set to None
  - I searched for a Legend.__getstate__ override and found none.
  - Default pickling for artists is implemented in Artist.__getstate__:
    - artist.py:216-221: def __getstate__(self): d = self.__dict__.copy(); d['stale_callback'] = None; return d
- Conclusion: The draft claim is correct. Legend stores the helper on self._draggable and does not override __getstate__, so the default Artist.__getstate__ will include _draggable in the pickled dict unless Legend removes it (it does not).

3) Verify Text/Annotation pattern and Text.__getstate__ behavior
- Why I checked: The draft claims draggable Annotations use the same pattern and Text.__getstate__ does not clear _draggable.
- What I inspected:
  - _AnnotationBase.__init__ initializes self._draggable = None:
    - text.py:1467: self._draggable = None
  - Annotation.draggable toggles the helper and assigns DraggableAnnotation to self._draggable:
    - text.py:1643-1650: if state: if self._draggable is None: self._draggable = DraggableAnnotation(self, use_blit); else disconnect and set None
  - Text.__getstate__ currently only clears the cached renderer:
    - text.py:235-239: def __getstate__(self): d = super().__getstate__(); d['_renderer'] = None; return d
  - There is no handling in Text.__getstate__ that clears or disconnects _draggable.
- Conclusion: The draft claim is correct. Draggable annotations follow the same helper pattern, and Text.__getstate__ does not remove or disconnect the _draggable helper.

4) Verify Figure.__getstate__ behavior and insufficiency of popping only Figure.canvas
- Why I checked: The draft claims Figure.__getstate__ pops the 'canvas' key but this does not eliminate indirect references to the canvas held by helpers on artists.
- What I inspected:
  - Figure.__getstate__ pops the canvas from the Figure state:
    - figure.py:3160-3166: state = super().__getstate__(); state.pop("canvas"); ... return state
  - The code does not (and cannot easily) traverse all artists to remove references elsewhere (and we observe helpers in artists refer to canvas).
- Conclusion: The draft claim is correct. Removing Figure.canvas from Figure state does not remove other references (e.g., Draggable helper objects stored on artists), so pickling may still attempt to pickle the FigureCanvas via those references.

5) Verify whether any other similar locations exist
- Why I checked: The draft notes the same root cause manifests in both Legend and Annotation/Text. I searched the codebase for other uses of "_draggable" and Draggable helper subclasses to ensure no other locations were missed.
- What I inspected:
  - Search for "_draggable" across lib/matplotlib/*.py returned hits in legend.py and text.py only (plus the initializers).
  - Draggable helper classes are defined in offsetbox.py (DraggableBase and subclasses DraggableOffsetBox and DraggableAnnotation) and DraggableLegend is in legend.py (it subclasses DraggableOffsetBox).
- Conclusion: The same problematic pattern occurs in Legend (DraggableLegend -> DraggableOffsetBox -> DraggableBase) and in Annotation (DraggableAnnotation -> DraggableBase). No additional classes were found with the same _draggable pattern.

6) Check for any unsupported or overreaching claims in the draft
- The draft makes the following claims; all are supported by the code:
  - DraggableBase assigns self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas and registers callbacks with _connect_picklable -> supported (offsetbox.py:1508,1511-1515).
  - Legend stores the helper in self._draggable and has no __getstate__ -> supported (legend.py:614,1196-1205; no __getstate__ in legend.py; Artist.__getstate__ exists).
  - Text.__getstate__ clears only _renderer and does not handle _draggable -> supported (text.py:235-239 and text.py:1643-1650).
  - Figure.__getstate__ pops canvas but does not clear indirect references -> supported (figure.py:3160-3166).
- The draft does not assert anything about a particular backend being always used; it correctly states an example backend (FigureCanvasQTAgg) as a typical non-picklable backend object. That is consistent with the TypeError observed (cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object).

7) Findings from the Investigation analysis that the draft missed or did not emphasize
- The investigation explicitly noted a user-visible consequence: pickling will not preserve draggability unless helpers are re-created on unpickling. The draft report focuses on the immediate pickling failure and root cause; it does not state that draggability persistence is an additional UX consideration for any remedy. This is not a root-cause item but is an important related finding (not strictly required in RootCause).
- The draft did not include any proposed code changes in the bug report fields (good — the report must remain a diagnosis). The investigation included a minimal fix strategy; that belongs in the investigation and not in the diagnosis.

8) Field discipline and structure
- Description: The draft Description concisely describes the symptom and the reason in one sentence. It is consistent with the code.
- RootCause: The draft lists four numbered findings (DraggableBase, Legend._draggable, Text/Annotation, Figure.__getstate__). These are accurate and correspond to the code. Per the guideline, RootCause should state the incorrect assumptions and locations. The draft does this. I will adjust wording slightly to make each finding an explicit "incorrect assumption" followed by the location(s) and a short statement of what is wrong. I will not add any fix suggestions in RootCause.
- ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior: both are concise and correct.
- StepsToReproduce: correspond to the original report and are fine.

Summary of verification
- All diagnostic claims in the draft are supported by the source code evidence.
- No claim in the draft was contradicted by the code.
- The draft omitted an explicit user-visible note that pickling currently cannot preserve draggability (i.e., unpickling will not re-create runtime helpers), which is an impact detail adjacent to the root cause; I will mention it as an observed implication in the evidence list (but not prescribe a fix in the report fields).

bug_report.Title: TypeError when pickling Figure with draggable Legend/Annotation (cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object)
bug_report.Description: Calling pickle.dumps(fig) on a Matplotlib Figure that contains a draggable Legend or draggable Annotation raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object. The failure occurs because runtime-only GUI objects (the Figure.canvas / FigureCanvas) are indirectly referenced by draggable helper objects attached to artists, and those helpers are included in the pickled state.
bug_report.RootCause:
1. Draggable helper objects capture and store a direct reference to the Figure's backend canvas (a runtime-only GUI object). Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableBase.__init__. Evidence (code patterns): self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas and the helper registers callbacks on this canvas (e.g. self.canvas.callbacks._connect_picklable(...)). Incorrect assumption: it is safe for a helper attached to an Artist to retain a direct reference to a non-picklable GUI canvas and therefore be included in the Artist's pickled state.

2. Legend stores the draggable helper on the Legend instance and does not exclude it from pickling. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend (constructor and set_draggable). Evidence (code patterns): self._draggable = None (constructor) and set_draggable assigns self._draggable = DraggableLegend(...) when enabling draggability. Incorrect assumption: the default Artist pickling (Artist.__getstate__) will not include any runtime-only helper fields, so no special handling is needed in Legend.

3. Annotation/Text uses the same pattern and its pickling logic does not remove the helper. Location: lib/matplotlib/text.py:_AnnotationBase and Text.__getstate__. Evidence (code patterns): _AnnotationBase initializes self._draggable and Annotation.draggable assigns DraggableAnnotation(self, ...), while Text.__getstate__ only clears the cached renderer (d['_renderer'] = None) and does not clear or disconnect an existing d['_draggable']. Incorrect assumption: Text/Annotation pickling need only clear renderer caches and does not need to remove runtime-only helpers.

4. Figure.__getstate__ removes only the Figure.canvas attribute but this is insufficient to prevent the canvas from being pickled indirectly. Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__getstate__. Evidence (code patterns): Figure.__getstate__ does state.pop("canvas") but does not remove references to the canvas that may exist on other objects (artists' runtime helpers). Incorrect assumption: removing the Figure.canvas key from the Figure state guarantees that no FigureCanvas objects will be encountered during pickling of a Figure and its artists.
StepsToReproduce: Run a Python script with Matplotlib (example environment: Windows 10, Matplotlib 3.7.0, Python 3.10).
Create a Figure and Axes, plot some data and add a legend.
Make the legend draggable: leg = ax.legend(); leg.set_draggable(True).
Call pickle.dumps(fig) (or otherwise pickle the Figure).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Calling pickle.dumps(fig) on a Figure with a draggable legend or draggable annotation succeeds (the Figure is serialized) and does not raise a TypeError related to the backend FigureCanvas object.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object when a Legend or Annotation has been made draggable because a helper attached to the artist holds a direct reference to the Figure canvas and that helper is included in the pickled state.

class Grouper:
    """
    A disjoint-set data structure.

    Objects can be joined using :meth:`join`, tested for connectedness
    using :meth:`joined`, and all disjoint sets can be retrieved by
    using the object as an iterator.

    The objects being joined must be hashable and weak-referenceable.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from matplotlib.cbook import Grouper
    >>> class Foo:
    ...     def __init__(self, s):
    ...         self.s = s
    ...     def __repr__(self):
    ...         return self.s
    ...
    >>> a, b, c, d, e, f = [Foo(x) for x in 'abcdef']
    >>> grp = Grouper()
    >>> grp.join(a, b)
    >>> grp.join(b, c)
    >>> grp.join(d, e)
    >>> list(grp)
    [[a, b, c], [d, e]]
    >>> grp.joined(a, b)
    True
    >>> grp.joined(a, c)
    True
    >>> grp.joined(a, d)
    False
    """

    def __init__(self, init=()):
        self._mapping = {weakref.ref(x): [weakref.ref(x)] for x in init}

    def __contains__(self, item):
        return weakref.ref(item) in self._mapping

    def clean(self):
        """Clean dead weak references from the dictionary."""
        mapping = self._mapping
        to_drop = [key for key in mapping if key() is None]
        for key in to_drop:
            val = mapping.pop(key)
            val.remove(key)

    def join(self, a, *args):
        """
        Join given arguments into the same set.  Accepts one or more arguments.
        """
        mapping = self._mapping
        set_a = mapping.setdefault(weakref.ref(a), [weakref.ref(a)])

        for arg in args:
            set_b = mapping.get(weakref.ref(arg), [weakref.ref(arg)])
            if set_b is not set_a:
                if len(set_b) > len(set_a):
                    set_a, set_b = set_b, set_a
                set_a.extend(set_b)
                for elem in set_b:
                    mapping[elem] = set_a

        self.clean()

    def joined(self, a, b):
        """Return whether *a* and *b* are members of the same set."""
        self.clean()
        return (self._mapping.get(weakref.ref(a), object())
                is self._mapping.get(weakref.ref(b)))

    def remove(self, a):
        self.clean()
        set_a = self._mapping.pop(weakref.ref(a), None)
        if set_a:
            set_a.remove(weakref.ref(a))

    def __iter__(self):
        """
        Iterate over each of the disjoint sets as a list.

        The iterator is invalid if interleaved with calls to join().
        """
        self.clean()
        unique_groups = {id(group): group for group in self._mapping.values()}
        for group in unique_groups.values():
            yield [x() for x in group]

    def get_siblings(self, a):
        """Return all of the items joined with *a*, including itself."""
        self.clean()
        siblings = self._mapping.get(weakref.ref(a), [weakref.ref(a)])
        return [x() for x in siblings]


class GrouperView:
    """Immutable view over a `.Grouper`."""

    def __init__(self, grouper): self._grouper = grouper
    def __contains__(self, item): return item in self._grouper
    def __iter__(self): return iter(self._grouper)
    def joined(self, a, b): return self._grouper.joined(a, b)
    def get_siblings(self, a): return self._grouper.get_siblings(a)


def simple_linear_interpolation(a, steps):
    """
    Resample an array with ``steps - 1`` points between original point pairs.

    Along each column of *a*, ``(steps - 1)`` points are introduced between
    each original values; the values are linearly interpolated.

    Parameters
    ----------
    a : array, shape (n, ...)
    steps : int

    Returns
    -------
    array
        shape ``((n - 1) * steps + 1, ...)``
    """
    fps = a.reshape((len(a), -1))
    xp = np.arange(len(a)) * steps
    x = np.arange((len(a) - 1) * steps + 1)
    return (np.column_stack([np.interp(x, xp, fp) for fp in fps.T])
            .reshape((len(x),) + a.shape[1:]))

def _prevent_rasterization(draw):
    # We assume that by default artists are not allowed to rasterize (unless
    # its draw method is explicitly decorated). If it is being drawn after a
    # rasterized artist and it has reached a raster_depth of 0, we stop
    # rasterization so that it does not affect the behavior of normal artist
    # (e.g., change in dpi).

    @wraps(draw)
    def draw_wrapper(artist, renderer, *args, **kwargs):
        if renderer._raster_depth == 0 and renderer._rasterizing:
            # Only stop when we are not in a rasterized parent
            # and something has been rasterized since last stop.
            renderer.stop_rasterizing()
            renderer._rasterizing = False

        return draw(artist, renderer, *args, **kwargs)

    draw_wrapper._supports_rasterization = False
    return draw_wrapper


def allow_rasterization(draw):
    """
    Decorator for Artist.draw method. Provides routines
    that run before and after the draw call. The before and after functions
    are useful for changing artist-dependent renderer attributes or making
    other setup function calls, such as starting and flushing a mixed-mode
    renderer.
    """

    @wraps(draw)
    def draw_wrapper(artist, renderer):
        try:
            if artist.get_rasterized():
                if renderer._raster_depth == 0 and not renderer._rasterizing:
                    renderer.start_rasterizing()
                    renderer._rasterizing = True
                renderer._raster_depth += 1
            else:
                if renderer._raster_depth == 0 and renderer._rasterizing:
                    # Only stop when we are not in a rasterized parent
                    # and something has be rasterized since last stop
                    renderer.stop_rasterizing()
                    renderer._rasterizing = False

            if artist.get_agg_filter() is not None:
                renderer.start_filter()

            return draw(artist, renderer)
        finally:
            if artist.get_agg_filter() is not None:
                renderer.stop_filter(artist.get_agg_filter())
            if artist.get_rasterized():
                renderer._raster_depth -= 1
            if (renderer._rasterizing and artist.figure and
                    artist.figure.suppressComposite):
                # restart rasterizing to prevent merging
                renderer.stop_rasterizing()
                renderer.start_rasterizing()

    draw_wrapper._supports_rasterization = True
    return draw_wrapper


def _finalize_rasterization(draw):
    """
    Decorator for Artist.draw method. Needed on the outermost artist, i.e.
    Figure, to finish up if the render is still in rasterized mode.
    """
    @wraps(draw)
    def draw_wrapper(artist, renderer, *args, **kwargs):
        result = draw(artist, renderer, *args, **kwargs)
        if renderer._rasterizing:
            renderer.stop_rasterizing()
            renderer._rasterizing = False
        return result
    return draw_wrapper

    def __getstate__(self):
        # return list of groups with strong refs to objects

However, pickling of Figure is handled in Figure.__getstate__/__setstate__ in lib/matplotlib/figure.py:
- Figure.__getstate__ returns state = super().__getstate__() (Artist.__getstate__).
- Artist.__getstate__ simply copies the instance __dict__ with d = self.__dict__.copy() and replaces stale_callback with None (so stale_callback not pickled).
- Therefore, Figure pickling picks up any attribute stored in Figure.__dict__ — including self._align_label_groups (a dict with 'x' and 'y' pointing to cbook.Grouper instances). Those Grouper instances are pickled by the pickling machinery. Since Grouper._mapping contains weakref.refs that are not picklable, pickling fails.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (high-level)
- Make cbook.Grouper picklable by implementing __getstate__ and __setstate__ that serialize and restore group membership using picklable strong references (i.e., serialize each group as a list of the actual member objects). On unpickling, reconstruct the internal weakref.ref-based mapping from the lists of objects.

Concretely, add the following methods to class Grouper in lib/matplotlib/cbook.py (near its other methods):

    def __getstate__(self):
        # Clean dead refs and serialize as list of groups of strong refs
        self.clean()
        unique_groups = {id(group): group for group in self._mapping.values()}
        groups = []
        for group in unique_groups.values():
            members = [ref() for ref in group if ref() is not None]
            if members:
                groups.append(members)
        return {"groups": groups}

    def __setstate__(self, state):
        groups = state.get("groups", [])
        self._mapping = {}
        for group in groups:
            refs = [weakref.ref(x) for x in group]
            for r in refs:
                self._mapping[r] = refs

Why this fixes the bug
- Before: pickling Figure tried to pickle cbook.Grouper._mapping that contained weakref.ref() objects (ReferenceType), which raises TypeError because weakref.ref objects are not picklable.
- After: Grouper.__getstate__ returns a plain dict containing Python lists of the actual objects (Axes etc.), which are picklable as part of the Figure pickling graph. On unpickling, __setstate__ reconstructs the weakref.ref-based internal _mapping expected by the rest of the code.

Now the required challenge questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence answer: The current code assumes that internal weakref.ref objects inside Grouper (weakref.ReferenceType values) can be left in the instance dict and will not be pickled or that pickling will ignore them; the fix changes the assumption to: Grouper must control its pickled representation so that weakref.ref objects are not emitted directly into the pickle stream (i.e., groups are serialized as simple lists of strong refs).

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The Grouper code had no numeric hardcoded constants related to this issue. The implicit "assumption" is not encoded as a numeric constant but as a structural design: self._mapping stores weakref.ref keys and list-of-weakref.ref values. There are no literal hardcoded numbers/offsets in Grouper that depend on the assumption being true.
- Therefore, no numeric constants need to become dynamic.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- Complementary operation is unpickling: Figure.__setstate__ receives a state that includes the Grouper state. Because we add Grouper.__setstate__, unpickling will call Grouper.__setstate__ with the serialized groups list. Let's trace the unpickle flow concretely:
  - During pickling, Grouper.__getstate__ returns {"groups": [[ax1, ax2], [ax3], ...]}
  - During unpickling, Python first creates the figure and its child objects (Axes instances) as part of the same pickle graph. The Axes objects are constructed and assigned; their identity is preserved by the pickler. When the pickler reaches the Grouper object, it creates a new Grouper instance and then calls __setstate__ with the state dict. The group lists contain references to the unpickled Axes objects (the pickler provides the actual objects).
  - __setstate__ then builds weakref.ref(x) objects for each member x in the group list and reconstructs self._mapping as expected by the code.
  - Therefore, the reverse operation is correct: the pickled strong references to Axes are resolved to the actual Axes on unpickle, and weakrefs are reconstructed.
- Now check parent's methods: Figure.__setstate__ assigns self.__dict__ = state and then calls FigureCanvasBase(self) to set canvas etc. But importantly Figure.__setstate__ doesn't remove or otherwise touch _align_label_groups; that’s fine because Grouper.__setstate__ reconstructs _mapping properly. No change needed in Figure.__setstate__.
- One subtlety to verify: does Grouper.__setstate__ run before Axes __setstate__? It will not matter because the pickle memo contains references and the unpickler rebuilds objects as shared. The objects referenced in the group lists are references to the Axes objects (they're not unpickled as simple names), so by the time __setstate__ is called, the referenced objects exist as Python object instances (the unpickling machinery guarantees that the referenced objects are created as placeholders and filled in) — constructing weakref.ref(x) will succeed and point to the correct object.
- Therefore, the reverse operation is supported and consistent: we serialize groups via the actual objects, and upon unpickling we reconstruct the weakref mapping.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Examine each method of Grouper:
  - __init__(self, init=()): unchanged; still creates a mapping from weakref.ref(x) to list-of-weakref.ref(x) for x in init. Compatible with new __setstate__.
  - __contains__(self, item): uses weakref.ref(item) to look up mapping. Still valid — mapping reconstructed in __setstate__ will contain weakref.ref keys as before.
  - clean(self): unchanged — still removes dead weakrefs.
  - join(self, a, *args): unchanged — continues to put weakref.ref(a) keys and lists into mapping; __getstate__ will serialize the state from these lists as strong refs.
  - joined(self, a, b), remove(self, a), __iter__(self), get_siblings(self,a): all operate on the weakref-based mapping and will work unchanged. After unpickling, __setstate__ ensures mapping is in expected format (weakref.ref keys and list-of-weakrefs values).
- Therefore, existing methods continue to work.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- The new methods being added are exactly the __getstate__ and __setstate__ methods on Grouper. No additional methods are required elsewhere.
- We must also check other classes that expect to pickle Grouper-like things: There are no other special complementary operations required. The Axis code calls grouper.get_siblings and other methods; since we reconstruct the mapping, Axis code is fine.
- Check Figure.__getstate__/__setstate__: They do not special-case _align_label_groups. With Grouper now picklable, no change is required in Figure.
- Check CallbackRegistry pattern: CallbackRegistry also implements __getstate__/__setstate__ for pickling callbacks — we are following the same pattern by giving Grouper explicit pickling semantics.

Checkpoint 5: Final report and concrete patch

Root cause
- cbook.Grouper stores weakref.ref objects directly in its instance state (self._mapping: mapping weakref.ref -> [weakref.ref, ...]). weakref.ref instances (weakref.ReferenceType) are not picklable by default, so pickling a Figure with populated align_label_groups (Grouper instances with weakrefs) raises TypeError when pickle attempts to serialize the Grouper._mapping.

Fix summary (complete)
- Implement __getstate__ and __setstate__ for matplotlib.cbook.Grouper to serialize group membership as lists of the actual referenced objects (which are picklable within the Figure's pickling graph) and to reconstruct the internal weakref-based mapping on unpickle.

Files to change
- lib/matplotlib/cbook.py — modify class Grouper by adding the two methods described below.

Concrete code to add (diff-style explanation)
- In lib/matplotlib/cbook.py, inside class Grouper, add the following methods (place them after get_siblings or after __iter__ — anywhere inside the class is fine). Exact code:

    def __getstate__(self):
        """
        Return picklable state for the Grouper.

        We cannot pickle weakref.ref objects directly, so serialize the
        groups as lists of the actual objects (strong refs). On unpickling,
        __setstate__ will rebuild the weakref.ref-based mapping.
        """
        # Ensure there are no dead weakrefs.
        self.clean()
        # unique_groups: get unique group lists
        unique_groups = {id(group): group for group in self._mapping.values()}
        groups = []
        for group in unique_groups.values():
            members = [ref() for ref in group if ref() is not None]
            if members:
                groups.append(members)
        return {"groups": groups}

    def __setstate__(self, state):
        """
        Restore the Grouper from the pickled state generated by __getstate__.
        The stored state is a dict with key "groups" mapping to a list of lists
        of objects. Reconstruct self._mapping as weakref.ref(obj) -> list-of-weakref.
        """
        groups = state.get("groups", [])
        self._mapping = {}
        for group in groups:
            refs = [weakref.ref(x) for x in group]
            for r in refs:
                self._mapping[r] = refs

Why this is the complete fix (addresses every affected location)
- The only place Figure stores Grouper instances is FigureBase._align_label_groups. Once Grouper supports a picklable state that doesn't leak non-picklable weakref.ref objects, pickling Figure works correctly.
- Axis and Figure code read/write Grouper only via Grouper.join, get_siblings, etc.; those methods continue to operate on the internal weakref-based mapping, which is reconstructed on unpickle. No changes required to Axis or Figure.
- CallbackRegistry already implements pickling semantics for callbacks; the proposed change follows a similar pattern: intercept pickling for an object that contains non-picklable internals and present a picklable view.

Potential caveats and rationale
- The new pickled representation stores strong references to objects (Axes) only transiently inside the pickle stream; this is normal and appropriate: those objects are part of the Figure pickling graph and will be pickled anyway. On unpickle, we rebuild weakrefs.
- If some group contained objects that are not picklable (rare in this use-case; axes objects are picklable as part of figure), those objects would cause pickling to fail for reasons unrelated to weakrefs; that behavior is unchanged.
- We clean dead weakrefs before pickling, avoiding including None references.
- We keep the exact internal structure (weakref.ref-based mapping) intact on unpickle so that existing Grouper methods remain valid.

Minimal patch / location to edit
- Edit lib/matplotlib/cbook.py: insert __getstate__ and __setstate__ into class Grouper.

Example verification (user reproduction)

    import pickle

    ax1 = fig.add_subplot(211)
    ax2 = fig.add_subplot(212)

    acc=[10,11,12,13,14]

    ax1.set_ylabel('speed')
    ax2.plot(time,acc)
    ax2.set_ylabel('acc')

    fig.align_labels()
    pickle.dumps(fig)   # should no longer raise TypeError

    plt.show()

- Expected: pickle.dumps(fig) should not raise TypeError anymore.

Summary of all locations that need to change
- Only lib/matplotlib/cbook.py (class Grouper): add __getstate__ and __setstate__ as above.
- No other files need modification: Figure / Axis code remain correct; they rely on the Grouper instance and its API.

If you want, I can:

compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: After calling Figure.align_labels(), pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType' object. The wrong assumption is that objects stored inside Figure.__dict__ (specifically the align-label grouping structure) contain only picklable values.
- Root cause: cbook.Grouper stores weakref.ref objects directly in its instance state (self._mapping uses weakref.ref keys and list-of-weakref.ref values). These weakref.ReferenceType objects are not picklable. (Location: lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::Grouper).
- Where the problem surfaces: FigureBase creates self._align_label_groups = {"x": cbook.Grouper(), "y": cbook.Grouper()} and Figure.align_labels() populates those Grouper instances (lib/matplotlib/figure.py). Artist.__getstate__ copies the instance __dict__ (so _align_label_groups is included) and does not sanitize weakrefs (lib/matplotlib/artist.py).
- Fix strategy: Make cbook.Grouper implement __getstate__ and __setstate__ to serialize group membership as picklable lists of the actual objects (strong refs) and to reconstruct the internal weakref.ref-based mapping on unpickle. This fixes pickling at the source and covers every consumer, because Figure and Axis use the Grouper API (join/get_siblings) only.
- Hardcoded values: none need changing. The issue is structural (weakrefs in instance state), not numeric configuration.
- Methods to add: cbook.Grouper.__getstate__ and cbook.Grouper.__setstate__ (no changes required to Figure, Axis, or Artist).
- Investigation gaps: I did not run the code or tests; I did not verify unpickle ordering by executing an unpickle cycle, nor added an automated unit test to the repo. Those are straightforward follow-ups but not blockers to the fix.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: align_labels populates a Grouper with weakrefs (Checkpoint 1)
What: FigureBase.align_labels / align_xlabels / align_ylabels call cbook.Grouper.join and therefore populate Grouper instances stored on the Figure.  
Why it matters: Those Grouper instances end up in Figure.__dict__ (self._align_label_groups) and thus are included in pickle.dumps(fig).  
Evidence: lib/matplotlib/figure.py creates self._align_label_groups = {"x": cbook.Grouper(), "y": cbook.Grouper()} and later calls self._align_label_groups['y'].join(ax, axc) (Figure.align_ylabels). (Produced at Checkpoint 1)

### Finding 2: Grouper stores raw weakref.ReferenceType objects (root cause) (Checkpoint 1 & 2)
What: cbook.Grouper._mapping uses weakref.ref(...) both as dict keys and as list elements representing groups. Those weakref.ref objects are not picklable.  
Why it matters: Pickling an object graph that includes weakref.ReferenceType objects raises TypeError. This matches the user's stack trace.  
Evidence: lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::Grouper.__init__ and join create and store weakref.ref(x) and lists of such refs. (Produced at Checkpoint 1 and verified by reading cbook.py in Checkpoint 2)

### Finding 3: Artist.__getstate__ copies __dict__ so Figure pickling includes Grouper (Checkpoint 3)
What: Artist.__getstate__ does a shallow copy of self.__dict__ and leaves most attributes intact (only special-cases stale_callback).  
Why it matters: Figure pickling includes _align_label_groups unchanged, so the unpicklable weakref.refs in Grouper propagate into the pickle attempt.  
Evidence: lib/matplotlib/artist.py: Artist.__getstate__ returns a copy of __dict__ (observed in file). (Produced at Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 4: There is an established pattern for this kind of fix (Checkpoint 2)
What: CallbackRegistry in cbook implements __getstate__ / __setstate__ to serialize only picklable callbacks.  
Why it matters: Use the same pattern for Grouper: control pickled representation to avoid non-picklable internals.  
Evidence: lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::CallbackRegistry defines __getstate__ and __setstate__. (Produced at Checkpoint 2)

### Finding 5: Fix surface area is small — only Grouper needs explicit pickling (Checkpoint 3)
What: Figure and Axis call into Grouper via its public API (join/get_siblings). Reconstructing Grouper internal state on unpickle preserves all existing APIs.  
Why it matters: A localized change (Grouper.__getstate__/__setstate__) is sufficient; no other modules need edits.  
Evidence: Usage search showed only Figure and Axis interact with _align_label_groups (lib/matplotlib/figure.py and lib/matplotlib/axis.py). (Produced at Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 6: Gaps to follow-up (Checkpoint 4)
What: I did not run the pickle/unpickle cycle or automated tests; I did not add a unit test to confirm behavior across different pickling scenarios (e.g., partial graphs).  
Why it matters: On-disk verification ensures no subtle ordering or edge-case unpickling issues remain. Add test that calls fig.align_labels(), pickle.dumps(fig), pickle.loads(...), and exercises axis.get_siblings and Figure drawing. (Produced at Checkpoint 4)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: lib/matplotlib/cbook.py: G rouper (class Grouper)
- **Wrong assumption**: Grouper assumes storing weakref.ref objects directly in its instance state is safe for all consumers (including pickle).
- **Evidence**: Grouper._mapping is constructed like {weakref.ref(x): [weakref.ref(x)] ...} (see Grouper.__init__, join and other methods in lib/matplotlib/cbook.py). weakref.ReferenceType objects are not picklable — the user's TypeError precisely cites that type.
- **Related locations**:
  - lib/matplotlib/figure.py: FigureBase._align_label_groups stores Grouper instances on the Figure.
  - lib/matplotlib/axis.py: Axis methods read from Grouper via get_siblings.

### Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py: FigureBase.__init__ (attribute: _align_label_groups)
- **Wrong assumption**: Figure can include any object in its __dict__ and rely on default Artist pickling semantics to be correct (i.e., no special sanitization of internal helpers is required).
- **Evidence**: FigureBase.__init__ assigns self._align_label_groups = {"x": cbook.Grouper(), "y": cbook.Grouper()} and Figure.align_labels fills those Grouper instances (lib/matplotlib/figure.py lines establishing and populating _align_label_groups).
- **Related locations**:
  - lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::Grouper (the Grouper instance placed here).
  - lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.__getstate__ (which copies __dict__, causing the weakrefs to be included in pickling).

### Location: lib/matplotlib/artist.py: Artist.__getstate__
- **Wrong assumption**: Copying self.__dict__ is sufficient; no further sanitization is needed for attributes that may contain non-picklable internals.
- **Evidence**: Artist.__getstate__ creates d = self.__dict__.copy() and only replaces stale_callback. That means _align_label_groups (with its weakrefs) will be included verbatim when pickling a Figure.
- **Related locations**:
  - lib/matplotlib/figure.py::Figure (which inherits Artist and thus uses this pickling behavior).
  - lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::Grouper (whose internal weakref refs cause the TypeError).

--- end of report ---

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft report against the source code and the compressed investigation analysis. Below I list what I checked, why I checked it, the relevant code excerpts, and the conclusions. I include any gaps I found (missing locations or unsupported claims) and how I will reflect them in the revised report.

1) Verify cbook.Grouper implementation (claims about weakrefs and missing custom pickling)
- Why I inspected this file: The draft RootCause claims that cbook.Grouper stores weakref.ReferenceType objects in its instance state and that these are included in pickling. I expect to find weakref.ref(...) used for keys and group elements and no __getstate__/__setstate__ for Grouper.
- What I looked up: lib/matplotlib/cbook.py around the Grouper definition.
- Relevant code (quotes, line numbers from the repository):
  - class start and mapping construction:
    - lib/matplotlib/cbook.py:754-791:
      - "class Grouper:"
      - "__init__(self, init=()):"
      - "self._mapping = {weakref.ref(x): [weakref.ref(x)] for x in init}"
  - join() uses weakref.ref in setdefault/get and stores lists of weakrefs:
    - lib/matplotlib/cbook.py:802-816:
      - "set_a = mapping.setdefault(weakref.ref(a), [weakref.ref(a)])"
      - "set_b = mapping.get(weakref.ref(arg), [weakref.ref(arg)])"
      - "set_a.extend(set_b)"
      - "for elem in set_b: mapping[elem] = set_a"
  - get_siblings and iterator return the dereferenced objects, but the internal storage remains lists of weakrefs:
    - lib/matplotlib/cbook.py:832-847:
      - "__iter__: yield [x() for x in group]"
      - "get_siblings(self, a): ... siblings = self._mapping.get(weakref.ref(a), [weakref.ref(a)]) ; return [x() for x in siblings]"

- What I learned and conclusions:
  - The Grouper class stores weakref.ref objects directly in self._mapping (keys and list elements). The code confirms this explicitly.
  - The Grouper class does not define __getstate__ or __setstate__ within the class body (the class ends at get_siblings and is followed by GrouperView). There is no custom pickling handling in Grouper found in this file.
  - Therefore the draft claim that Grouper stores weakref.ReferenceType objects directly and that these are unpicklable is supported by the code.

2) Verify FigureBase._align_label_groups and align_labels usage (claims about storing Grouper on Figure and populating it)
- Why I inspected this file: The draft claims FigureBase places cbook.Grouper instances into the Figure state (self._align_label_groups) and that Figure.align_labels populates them, thereby adding non-picklable weakrefs to the Figure __dict__.
- What I looked up: lib/matplotlib/figure.py for initialization and align_labels code paths.
- Relevant code (quotes, line numbers):
  - Creation of groups in FigureBase.__init__:
    - lib/matplotlib/figure.py:169-191:
      - "class FigureBase(Artist):"
      - "self._align_label_groups = {\"x\": cbook.Grouper(), \"y\": cbook.Grouper()}"
  - align_ylabels uses Grouper.join:
    - lib/matplotlib/figure.py:1439-1455:
      - Inside align_ylabels loop: "self._align_label_groups['y'].join(ax, axc)"
  - align_xlabels likewise calls join (search results showed similar lines for x at ~1394):
    - lib/matplotlib/figure.py:1394 (search hit) indicates analogous use for 'x' groups.

- What I learned and conclusions:
  - The code confirms that FigureBase constructs and stores two Grouper instances on the Figure at initialization.
  - The align_xlabels/align_ylabels implementation calls Grouper.join as the draft states, which will populate the Grouper's weakref-based _mapping.
  - This supports the draft claim that calling fig.align_labels() populates those Grouper instances which live on the Figure object and therefore become part of the Figure instance state.

3) Verify Artist.__getstate__ behavior (claims about copying __dict__ into pickled state)
- Why I inspected this file: The draft claims Artist.__getstate__ does a shallow copy of self.__dict__ and does not sanitize attributes that may contain non-picklable internals.
- What I looked up: lib/matplotlib/artist.py __getstate__ implementation.
- Relevant code (quotes, line numbers):
  - lib/matplotlib/artist.py:216-221:
    - "def __getstate__(self):"
    - "d = self.__dict__.copy()"
    - "d['stale_callback'] = None"
    - "return d"

- What I learned and conclusions:
  - Artist.__getstate__ indeed returns a shallow copy of the instance __dict__ with only stale_callback replaced by None. It does not otherwise remove or transform attributes that may be non-picklable.
  - Because Figure inherits from Artist, Artist.__getstate__ is used for Figure_pickling unless Figure overrides it; FigureBase does not override pickling here.
  - Therefore, Grouper instances assigned to Figure._align_label_groups will be included verbatim in the pickled state.

4) Verify Axis interaction with the Grouper (missing location in the draft's RootCause)
- Why I inspected this file: The investigation analysis includes Axis._get_tick_boxes_siblings as a consumer of the Grouper contents; the draft RootCause did not explicitly name Axis/axis.py. I need to confirm Axis reads from Figure._align_label_groups so it is an additional affected location that must be listed in RootCause.
- What I looked up: lib/matplotlib/axis.py _get_tick_boxes_siblings implementation.
- Relevant code (quotes, line numbers):

    - "... axis._get_ticklabel_bboxes(...)"
    - "return bboxes, bboxes2"

- What I learned and conclusions:
  - Axis._get_tick_boxes_siblings directly queries the Grouper instance stored on the Figure to get sibling Axes. This confirms Axis is another consumer of the Grouper.
  - Because Axis relies on the Grouper being present and populated, any pickling of a Figure that includes its Groupe rstate may affect pickling behavior and, upon unpickling, Axis methods expect the Grouper structure to be reconstructable.

bug_report.Title: Figure cannot be pickled after calling Figure.align_labels() (TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType')
bug_report.Description: Calling Figure.align_labels() populates internal label-alignment helpers and afterwards pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType' object. The error occurs when pickling the Figure object after label alignment has been performed.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::Grouper stores weakref.ReferenceType objects directly in its instance state (self._mapping). Evidence in the code: the Grouper constructor and join/get_siblings use weakref.ref(...) for mapping keys and for elements in group lists (e.g. self._mapping = {weakref.ref(x): [weakref.ref(x)] for x in init} and setdefault/get calls with weakref.ref(...)). weakref.ReferenceType objects are not picklable, so leaving them in an object's instance state makes pickling that object raise TypeError.

2) lib/matplotlib/figure.py::FigureBase places cbook.Grouper instances on the Figure instance (self._align_label_groups = {"x": cbook.Grouper(), "y": cbook.Grouper()}) and Figure.align_xlabels / Figure.align_ylabels populate those Grouper instances by calling Grouper.join(...). Because the Grouper objects are attributes of the Figure, their state is included when the Figure is pickled.

3) lib/matplotlib/axis.py::Axis._get_tick_boxes_siblings consumes the Grouper stored on the Figure (it reads from self.figure._align_label_groups and calls grouper.get_siblings(self.axes)). This shows Axis methods rely on the Grouper API and are part of the same root-cause pattern: the non-picklable weakref-based state stored in Grouper propagates through Figure into consumers such as Axis.

4) lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.__getstate__ returns a shallow copy of self.__dict__ (d = self.__dict__.copy(); d['stale_callback'] = None; return d) and does not otherwise sanitize attributes. Because Figure inherits Artist and does not override this pickling behavior for the _align_label_groups attribute, the Grouper instances — and therefore their internal weakref.ReferenceType objects — are included verbatim in the pickled state. The combination of (1) + (2) + (3) + (4) explains why pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError after align_labels() has populated the Grouper instances on the Figure.
StepsToReproduce: Create a figure and two subplots: fig = plt.figure(); ax1 = fig.add_subplot(211); ax2 = fig.add_subplot(212)
Plot data and set y-labels on both axes (so labels exist to align).
Call fig.align_labels()
Call pickle.dumps(fig) and observe the TypeError
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: pickle.dumps(fig) completes successfully and returns a pickle (bytes) representing the Figure after align_labels() without raising TypeError.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling pickle.dumps(fig) after fig.align_labels() raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType' object.

        if self.ignore(event) or event.canvas.mouse_grabber != self.ax:
            return
        event.canvas.release_mouse(self.ax)
        if self.eventson and event.inaxes == self.ax:
            self._observers.process('clicked', event)

    def _motion(self, event):
        if self.ignore(event):
            return
        c = self.hovercolor if event.inaxes == self.ax else self.color
        if not colors.same_color(c, self.ax.get_facecolor()):
            self.ax.set_facecolor(c)
            if self.drawon:
                if self._useblit:
                    self.ax.draw_artist(self.ax)
                    self.canvas.blit(self.ax.bbox)
                else:
                    self.canvas.draw()

    def on_clicked(self, func):
        """
        Connect the callback function *func* to button click events.

        Returns a connection id, which can be used to disconnect the callback.
        """
        return self._observers.connect('clicked', lambda event: func(event))

    def disconnect(self, cid):
        """Remove the callback function with connection id *cid*."""
        self._observers.disconnect(cid)


class SliderBase(AxesWidget):
    """
    The base class for constructing Slider widgets. Not intended for direct
    usage.

    For the slider to remain responsive you must maintain a reference to it.
    """
    def __init__(self, ax, orientation, closedmin, closedmax,
                 valmin, valmax, valfmt, dragging, valstep):
        if ax.name == '3d':
            raise ValueError('Sliders cannot be added to 3D Axes')

        super().__init__(ax)
        _api.check_in_list(['horizontal', 'vertical'], orientation=orientation)

        self.orientation = orientation
        self.closedmin = closedmin
        self.closedmax = closedmax
        self.valmin = valmin
        self.valmax = valmax
        self.valstep = valstep
        self.drag_active = False
        self.valfmt = valfmt

        if orientation == "vertical":
            ax.set_ylim((valmin, valmax))
            axis = ax.yaxis
        else:
            ax.set_xlim((valmin, valmax))
            axis = ax.xaxis

        self._fmt = axis.get_major_formatter()
        if not isinstance(self._fmt, ticker.ScalarFormatter):
            self._fmt = ticker.ScalarFormatter()
            self._fmt.set_axis(axis)
        self._fmt.set_useOffset(False)  # No additive offset.
        self._fmt.set_useMathText(True)  # x sign before multiplicative offset.

        ax.set_axis_off()
        ax.set_navigate(False)

        self.connect_event("button_press_event", self._update)
        self.connect_event("button_release_event", self._update)
        if dragging:
            self.connect_event("motion_notify_event", self._update)
        self._observers = cbook.CallbackRegistry(signals=["changed"])

    def _stepped_value(self, val):
        """Return *val* coerced to closest number in the ``valstep`` grid."""
        if isinstance(self.valstep, Number):
            val = (self.valmin
                   + round((val - self.valmin) / self.valstep) * self.valstep)
        elif self.valstep is not None:
            valstep = np.asanyarray(self.valstep)
            if valstep.ndim != 1:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"valstep must have 1 dimension but has {valstep.ndim}"
                )
            val = valstep[np.argmin(np.abs(valstep - val))]
        return val

    def disconnect(self, cid):
        """
        Remove the observer with connection id *cid*.

        Parameters
        ----------
        cid : int
            Connection id of the observer to be removed.
        """
        self._observers.disconnect(cid)

    def reset(self):
        """Reset the slider to the initial value."""
        if np.any(self.val != self.valinit):
            self.set_val(self.valinit)

### Bug summary

When using clear figure, adding new widgets and then redrawing the current figure in the on_changed callback of a range slider the inputs to all the widgets in the figure are blocked. When doing the same in the button callback on_clicked, everything works fine.

### Code for reproduction

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as pyplot
import matplotlib.widgets as widgets

def onchanged(values):
    print("on changed")
    print(values)
    pyplot.clf()
    addElements()
    pyplot.draw()

    print("on click")
    pyplot.clf()
    addElements()
    pyplot.draw()

    ax = pyplot.axes([0.1, 0.45, 0.8, 0.1])
    global slider
    slider = widgets.RangeSlider(ax, "Test", valmin=1, valmax=10, valinit=(1, 10))
    slider.on_changed(onchanged)
    ax = pyplot.axes([0.1, 0.30, 0.8, 0.1])
    global button
    button = widgets.Button(ax, "Test")
    button.on_clicked(onclick)

addElements()

pyplot.show()
```

The widgets can't receive any input from a mouse click, when redrawing in the on_changed callback of a range Slider.

Proposed fix (high level)
- In FigureBase.delaxes (lib/matplotlib/figure.py) (the method that actually removes an Axes from its Figure), check if the figure's canvas.mouse_grabber is the axes that is about to be removed. If so, call self.canvas.release_mouse(ax) before removing the axes from the figure's ax stack. This ensures that if a widget holds the mouse grab at the time the axes is removed (for example when a RangeSlider triggers callbacks while being dragged), the canvas will be left in a consistent state and future widget events won't be blocked.

Concrete code patch (minimal, precise):

In lib/matplotlib/figure.py, in the delaxes method (before self._axstack.remove(ax)), add:

    # If this Axes currently holds the canvas mouse grab, release it so that
    # the canvas is not left with a stale mouse_grabber reference after the
    # Axes is removed.  This prevents widgets from being blocked if an Axes
    # is deleted while a widget has grabbed the mouse (e.g., a Slider being
    # dragged and the user code calling clf()).
    canvas = getattr(self, "canvas", None)
    if canvas is not None and getattr(canvas, "mouse_grabber", None) is ax:
        try:
            canvas.release_mouse(ax)
        except Exception:
            # Be defensive: do not let clearing axes raise due to backend oddities.
            pass

Place that immediately before the existing self._axstack.remove(ax) call in delaxes.

Patch context example (showing the relevant part of delaxes with the insertion):

    def delaxes(self, ax):
        ...
        # Break link between any twinned axes
        for name in ax._axis_names:
            last_ax = _break_share_link(ax, ax._shared_axes[name])
            if last_ax is not None:
                _reset_locators_and_formatters(last_ax._axis_map[name])

        # RELEASE THE MOUSE GRAB IF THIS AXES IS HOLDING IT.
        canvas = getattr(self, "canvas", None)
        if canvas is not None and getattr(canvas, "mouse_grabber", None) is ax:
            try:
                canvas.release_mouse(ax)
            except Exception:
                pass

        self._axstack.remove(ax)
        self._axobservers.process("_axes_change_event", self)
        self.stale = True
        self._localaxes.remove(ax)

Why this exact change:
- The canvas stores a reference to the currently mouse-grabbing Axes in canvas.mouse_grabber. If we remove that Axes without releasing the grab, the canvas retains a reference to a removed axes, and later interactions will see mouse_grabber not None and will not allow new axes to grab the mouse (or will raise RuntimeError), effectively blocking widget input. Releasing the grab when the held axes is about to be removed leaves canvas.mouse_grabber in a consistent None state.
